contents: Re: Botox Today I am glad you are getting some where. The elimination diets are so hard to do but so worth it. For breakfast I think real oats is good to try, then maybe cream of wheat, or grits, red river, for variety. The reason I think they are good option b/c they have very few ingredience. I drunk Coconut milk, plain unsweetened, I like it, but it us really easy to grab the wrong one and get one with vanilla and or sweetened. I can't do stevia, but it is a herb and I have lots of allergies to herbs. I will be counting your sleeps with you. I really hope it works this time. It sounds like the next few months will be tough, but after that I hope things will go smoothly for your. At least with being off work you have the time to fine tune all this. Eggs is a tough one, but it us do able. Cheers

contents: Re: Botox Today I am beyond happy! It seems to be working. The constant sensation to pee is gone, the urethral pain is intermittent, the constant bladder pain is diminished but still very present, the frequency has cut in half the last two days - YAY. I am peeing on my own but as the botox kicks in this could change - I could have to catheterize to pee as I may not be able to pee on my own. I am beyond happy! Praise God for giving me my life back :-) - I know it's coming :-)

I am hoping that this new diet that I am using will eventually help with pain. Have a blessed day everyone. It is instillation time for me. Many, many blessings :-)

contents: Well, I'm still not sure if it meets my expectations. My urgency and frequency and cut in half-but the retention is really horrible. I think I had a really strong dose (300 cc's) based on articles I've read and this message board. I don't know why my doctor does this much all in the bladder. My bladder pain is much less but I have pain in the urethra and abdominal muscles from straining to get the urine out. I'm scared I'm leaving too much urine in there and will get an infection. It's like being constipated in the other area. My uro says the retention will start to go away as it wears off. It's not bad enough to catheterize but my bathroom trips take 15 minutes. I really think I'm straining too hard.
signature: Indy

contents: What worked for me was to put my fingertips over the pubic bone and kind of milk out the urine. It's called "crede" in the nursing world. Didn't have to cath which I really can't handle, my urethra is a mess and didn't have to strain. Even though I'm way past the botox effects, I kept on crede-ing to keep totally empty especially before lying down because the emptier I can be, the longer my chances of staying down a few mins. are. Good luck.

contents: Botox And Urinary Retention Hi Indy, I have only had two Botox treatments - 200 units each time internally into the vagina to help with the pain in the pelvic muscles of which the bladder is a muscle. I noticed quickly that I had retention also because for me my problem is usually the opposite. If I were you, I might suggest you get checked out for a UTI. Since my retention went on for months, I kept getting infections and would have to go on Cipro to treat it. After I went for my second treatment on March 18 which was done in the hospital I became ill a few days later and was diagnosed with C.Difficile which is an intestinal bacterial infection that is caused by being on antibiotics and then being exposed to the bacteria in the hospital. So now I am on Vancomycin which is the strongest antibiotic we have. I had the C.D. three years ago and it took me more than ten months to get rid of the infection. I have heard about a drug called Cardura that might help with the retention if you wanted to ask your doctor about it. For me, I have decided to stop the Botox treatments because I can't risk getting UTIs and having to go on antibiotics. I have had the C.Difficile infection three times now and in 2002 I nearly died from the effect it had on my heart due to dehydration and my intestines almost rupturing. I will just be glad to get rid of the C.Difficile and have that miserable pain out of my life. Hopefully then if I stop the Botox the UTIs will also stop and then I will deal with the IC somehow as I have tried practically all the treatments for it except removal of the bladder which my urologist said he will do for me like tomorrow if I decide I want to go that route. But I know that would not take care of all of my pain as I have had ten pelvic/abdominal surgeries so scar tissue is also a big part of my pain problem along with PFD. Good luck and I hope you feel better soon. LOUISE

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress Wow Louise! I have never read about serious side effects of Botox. That is why I have been working so hard to empty my bladder because I wasn't sure if I'd feel an infection. I have been getting checked for UTI's once a week and so far I've been clear. I am drinking a lot of water which if I save it up, seems to give the urine stream some force. I really think 300 cc's was a huge dose. I have had some really great pain free days-not every day but am adding back more foods and putting on some healthy weight I've needed after that stupid IC diet which didn't help and just gave me malnutrition. I really hope you don't ever have to get your bladder removed. I really think the pain we've had for years is a pain curcuit set up that is in the spinal cord-like RSD of the bladder (like when someone loses a limb and still has pain). Have you looked into nerve blocks? Not the Interstim. The last ICA conference had a lot of info on new pain treatments. Good luck. '

contents: Hi Indy, I think the retention will ease up soon. I had 200 units in my bladder the first round and straining made it even worst. Louise, I never heard of anything like what happended with you. I hope you feel better soon. Cindy

contents: Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress Hi. It's now been 6 weeks. I had 10 days of bliss, with the retention managable and almost no pain. I was eating a lot of things I had sworn off and peeing less than 10 times per day. Then, something went wrong. Is it possible to have flares on BOTOX? I don't have an infection, I'm checked once a week. I do have severe constipation. After I go, the spasms start up. Maybe I have added to many foods too soon. I'm really getting upset-after getting a taste of being normal again. Any one with any ideas please!

contents: Hi Indy, Botox is not a "get out of jail free card". If you eat things you are not use to eating, not only will your bladder let you know but so will your colon. Colon spasms can make you feel like you are having bladder spasms. I have been able to do more and eat more since Botox but in moderation. Somethings will still cause flares but they are much more manageable now. I know what will cause flares and pre-treat before the symptoms. Cindy

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress CINSIN, thanks for your reply. The first thing I am going to do is get off the elavil which was causing retention and constipation even before the botox. I guess I should have known better than to add to many foods back at once. I also added back calcium citrate and cystaq and think one of them could be bothering me. I thought the cystaq which I took for years before the 1 1/2 year flare would help my lining heal. So, you have given me hope that my Botox hasn't just worn off after 6 weeks and to get out of my denial that I am cured. It seems the most important thing is to prevent constipation. Thanks.

contents: All I take daily is Bactrim for reoccurring UTI's. Other then an antibiotic I take Vicodin as needed and Valium only to manage after sex flares or if I have to ride in a car for more then an 90 minutes. But I don't need both daily - just to manage my symptoms when needed. I took Elmiron three times and it didn't help but we have figured out that most of my pain is muscle, nerve and tissue pain. I probably have more vaginal and urethra pain and don't have much bladder irritation and burning. I work with support groups and have seen where Elmiron has worked miracles but mostly with those who have irritation and burning.

I take daily Atarax at night I have pudendal nerve pain too, so I was taking Neurontin, the problem was the head ache that I had everyday so I´m taking Lyrica (is better and soft than Neurontin, and the maker is Pfizer too), the prooblem with this is that causes me retention, so I try to take only with pain I have irritation and burning inside the bladder so I have heparin and xilocain instilations too. And I use to take Bladuril (I don´t know the name in USA)

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress I'm 2 1/2 months into the 300 cc directly into my bladder. I have had several flares lasting days and they increase the retention I already have. I went to get cathertized and after I thought I was empty, he got out 2 full beakers! The pain is from being full and staying in my bladder and irritating it. When will this retention wear off? I had thought I noticed it getting better until I had a big flare from eating parmasian chees by mistake. I've also had flares from constipation and straining. There are some really great stretches of time where I am so happy with it but I don't understand the flares! It's impossible to reach my doctor by phone and I don't have an appointment until next month. I'm going to see a pain doctor about an epidural.
signature: Indy

contents: Indy- Thanks for being honest - that's what I need to hear. I've been dealing with this crap (IC) for almost 4 years without a lot of help from meds, diet, etc. A year ago, I decided that maybe it was in my head, and I needed to learn to deal with it. I stopped going to Drs and started just trying to deal with it on my own (and with a lot of OTC meds). Now, I've realized what I knew all along - it's not in my head! I went back to my Uro (who, btw is a very compassionate person but I don't think he's very interested in IC) about a month ago, and he put me on one of the few antidepressants that I haven't taken. All it did was depress me - makes a lot of sense huh? I had heard that the Mayo Clinic was the place to go after you've tried everything else. So, I called them and they decided to take me as a patient. I'm going the end of the month and I'm very optimistic for the first time in several years. I know Botox is probably my next option, and I want to find out all I can about it. You mentioned something about an epidural. What's the deal with that? I didn't think epidural injections could be used in the pelvic/bladder area. If they can, do you mind sharing what you've learned about them?

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress I have been reading about some nerve blocks. I don't know if they will do anything to me until the Botox wears off. I'm thinking, maybe they can calm down some nerves that are still firing off and weren't affected by the botox since it was only in my bladder. I think 100 units would have been better for the first try. I can see how it does relieve the pain once the bladder is empty!
signature: Indy

contents: Indy - I had the same problem until my urologist put a permanent catherter in. It work a treat. I was going 4 or 5 times in a couple of hours, getting 100 -150 ml each time and then self cath and getting 600 ml. The permanent cath has made the pain ease considerably. Hope you find something that works.

I am so sorry that you both are having such a time with retention. I never had that much trouble with retention but never had that much injected in my bladder. It has really worked better for me to have 300 units total but less in my bladder and more in the urethra and pelvic floor. Flore, it didn't really help much to have my pudendal nerve injected. Cindy

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress CINCIN: How do they know exactly where to inject the pelvic floor? I have had trigger points injections there with marcaine with me telling the gyn where it hurt as she pushed on various spots. Does it affect sex? Wouldn't you know it, yesterday I had a great day and went out last night and didn't go for almost 4 hours with no pain.

Rose Fish, I see Dr. Smith in Houston and his nurse Susan told me the other day that she gave my email address to a lady in Canada, was it you? I only know one other lady who had injections in his office in her bladder. She said it hurt and unfortunately it didn't help her. That was before he started doing pelvic floor injections too and to me that makes a world of difference. Indy, The doctor either maps out the trigger points before the surgery or has the PT or gyn map out the trigger points for him. It really helps with pain during sex but I still have spasms after sex. The after sex flare isn't near as bad as before. Please feel free to email me at [email protected] if either of you have any more questions that I may be able to answer for you. Cindy

contents: How much Gabapentin do you find you need to take for it to work. Im on 200mg three times daily and it made no difference. I only got relief when I had the botox done and am wondering with the expence is the Gabapentin worth it?

Well, I had the injections last June 15th. My dr did the cysto/hydro too so I was in the hospital for 2 nights This time was incredible!!!!!!, I had a very big contraction in my pelvic floor, so in the surgery room, before I got sleep he checked me very carefully He talked with the anesthesiologyst about the pain after the hydrodistention and about the contraction in the pelvic floor, so they decided to get me sleep for 24 hours. Wow!!!!!!!!!!!!!! it was incredible, because I didn´t remember anything. My mom said that I was in pain may be for 5 hours after the surgery, But.......... I didn´t remember!!!!, I was so druggy (??), in fact the nurse took my a shower IN MY BED!!, I didn´t remember very well this either 2 days after the surgery, my dr removed the catheter and I went to the wc to pee. And what was my surprise that I COULD VOID WITHOUT PAIN AND RETENTION!!! He put 200 units: 50 in the trigone, and 150 in urethra, vagina and vulva (in the trigger points) I saw the video and I was in shock!! I saw how whe he injects the Botox and inmediatly a little bumb (??) is formed (??). He said that he put 25 shoots On Saturday I couldn´t sit, so I was in my bed, and on Sunday (In México was the father´s day) I went with my family to celebrate my husband I was pretty well. On Monday I went to my office. And today (1 week and 1 day after) I´m very very very well, my frequency is maybe 1 every 1 hour and at night I have to go to the wc maybe 3 times. I don´t have urgency and I´m totally pain free!!! I´m not taking neurontin now (because I don´t feel pain in my nerve) and I´m only taking dayly Atarax and TENS only 30 minutes at night Bye!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
I am so happy that you are doing so much better!
And you????
I am doing good.

Botox 4 weeks ago-my progress Hi ladies. I'm glad you are doing well. I am 3 months into this now. I have had some incredible days but still get flares like this week. I think it's related to the IBS. I will never get 300 units again. I still have retention, but only during the flares.

contents: I am interested in hearing from people who have tried Botox injections in their bladder or pelvic floor. It is something I am considering. I have read articles online about how Botox can be used to treat urgency and frequency (my main issue right now) but I have yet to see any current patient accounts. I went back and looked through older posts on this website about Botox experiences but I found most of the posts are at least 5 or 6 years old. Since Botox injections for the bladder is still fairly new, I was wondering if doctors are more successful in treating patients with Botox today then they were five years ago? In the past, when my doctor has recommended treatment options, I've always gone along with her recommendations without questioning it. This time, I want to be more proactive and make sure I am making an informed decision before I let someone stick poison in my bladder. Any experiences, both positive or negative would be appreciated! Thanks, Sabrina
I have read of people who had some success --- and some who had adverse reactions. I hope some who have tried this option will respond. Donna

contents: Botox I have had botox, and it is not something I would do again. It was one of my "last ditch efforts". Bled for several days after, suffered a lot of pain and MAD frequency for a good 2 weeks (typical for me to "go" around 25-30 times per day EVERY day). After botox, up to 60. This was a year and a half ago, my bladder has never been the same since. Sorry to have to post negative results, but it definitely did NOT work for me.

I had it done a few months ago & it took a few weeks to really notice a difference, but it did help for about 2 months. I had in done in both my bladder & pelvic floor muscles. I was not pain free, but I noticed that my bladder was not in a constant knot as well as my pelvic floor. It is a painful procedure to have done & i wouldnt ever have it done just in the office or anything...

I got it done about three weeks ago and have not felt the effects yet. I am participating in a study though, so I am not sure what dose or if I got placebo. But I wanted to describe the procedure a little. They just gave me a vicodin and some adavan for the procedure, they did not put me to sleep. They numbed up my bladder and urethra with lidocane, let it sit for about 15 min in there. They then distended the bladder a little, which I think is the worst, so uncomfortable. Then they stick something in your urethra that I guess is the needle for the injections. They did 20 different injections all over the bladder. It was really intense and felt like getting a tattoo inside my bladder. It was really painful but only lasted 5 min so it was tolerable. I was very uncomfortable after but not really from the injections, more from all the stuff they stuck in my urethra. Right now I feel like I am still recovering because I am still having heightened pain and it is difficult to actually pee. I have not gotten any relief from it yet, so not sure if I got the placebo or not. I would recommend it though because I feel like the procedure is really not that bad. I think the urodynamics was way worse. Although I cannot say anything about the actual drug yet because I am still feeling pain and am still on the pain medication. Please message me if you have any questions about it, I know that not very many people have actually done it so I would like to give any info I can to people. Thanks!

contents: I'd like to keep up to date on this post as well. I had a cysto done recently in the office as a check-up 3 years after being origionally diaonosed. My bladder looked ok, no inflammation, but I had alot of blood vessles present, which the doctor explained was why my bladder was overly sensitive, or "on alert" as he put it. Also, he discovered that my urethra was very closed, and had a hard time getting it to open up. He said this is what's causing the "pinching" feeling. My vulvo-vaginal specialist got this news, and wants me to consider botox for my urethra muscles only to see if that can calm it down. I'm very worried about this b/c my husband and I want to TTC in Jan, and I'm worried that it may send me into a flare, or cause leaking, or more retention, and I'll be in worse shape than I am now. I also can't find much info about this procedure, and my doc said it was new to her, and she was going to try on other patients first to see how they respond. At least I won't be the first, but I'm still nervous. Anyways...thanks for the great post!! I'm going to keep up on this one before i make a decision.

signature: I have IC, the IBS, Heartburn, High bloodpressure,thyrod problems, depression and clorties pain.

contents: Hi Sabrina I have been having Botox injections for a couple of years after all other treatments failed. The Botox isn't ideal but it has some benefit. Contrary to what all the doctors have told me should happen, it takes 3-4 1/2 months to start working with me. I then get about 4 months of a great result whereby my bladder is all but normal. By the 8 month mark it has worn off and I have Botox again. One problem is that I have a bit of trouble urinating for the first couple of months each time, and I have also had a paralysed bowel for a few weeks the last couple of times which has been treated with Sorbitol, a mild laxative. Each time I have the procedure, I am fine for the first month, and then I have about 6 weeks of severe disabling pain and frequency, followed by about another 6 weeks of lesser pain and frequency. The doctors have given me different amounts of Botox (150 - 300 units) each time. The effect of having 300 units has meant the 6 weeks of bad pain is even worse and the eventual benefit is no better or more prolonged so I will only have 200 units from now on. Even though I only have relief for 1/2 of the time, it is great to be able to plan holidays and events for the 4 month blocks that I know I'm going to be symptom-free and to be able to look forward to a period of such significant pain relief.

Numerous oral medications for the bladder, none of which helped. 4 treatments of Botox injections - minimal success. Permanent Interstim implanted August 2012 - nil benefit so removed May 2013 Bladder nerve cauterization March 2014 and April 2014

contents: Hi Sabrina, The paralysed bowel has meant that there is no sensation or urge to go so I get constipated. The problem varies a bit from week to week and wears off eventually. At the moment I am only having the laxatives once a week. Once at its worst I hadn't been for 8 days and had to take the preparation people are given before a colonscopy. The bowel problem didn't happen when I had the smaller amount of Botox which is what I will have from now on. The urologist said he had never seen anyone have bowel problems from Botox before. With the urinating difficulties, it too is worse with the higher Botox amount. I have never needed a catheter but when my bladder is bad and I need to go frequently in the night I find I might have to sit there for several minutes to get anything out, and sometimes nothing will come out. Then I might try successfully five minutes later. It is only a problem when I am passing very small amounts (a few drops). When the bladder has a bit more in it it doesn't happen. This problem also wears off before the Botox starts improving the problem. The pain and frequency is always worse 1 month after the Botox surgery and stays like that for a couple of months. Because the pattern is always the same I am assuming the Botox is causing it. As far as having the Botox goes, I have only ever had it under general anaesthetic and would never have it without as they give about 30 injections with the aid of a cystoscope to see what they are doing. Each time, for a few hours afterwards, I have to pass urine every 15 minutes or so, which hurts a bit, there is a little bit of bleeding, and then by the next day it has settled enough to resume normal activities.

contents: Ive had botox twice, just recently had the second round about 3 weeks ago...and id have to say the more botox you do, the more risk you have of going into retention. And id say the pain is worse for awhile after. and the botox usually doesnt show its side effects right away, usually a week or two. theres definitely a lot of pros and cons...sometimes u risk getting infections afterwards and the pain. just one of those things you have to decide.

Luckily I have not needed a catheter to pee. I will try to go, have nothing happen, get up and come back 5-10 min later, do this three times and the fourth time trying I will be able to go. Or if I can go the first try I will have to push really hard to get it all out. Right now it sucks because when I cannot go I still feel like I need to, with pressure and pain, so in the middle of the night I cannot get back to sleep until I can actually go. It does give me some hope that this might go away and I will feel good. Hope the same happens to me! Right now things are the same, pain and frequency, with trouble actually going thrown in. Although not sure if it is worth all the pain and trouble if it does end up helping for a little bit. I don't know! I just want to be normal!

contents: Hi, I don't have IC, but I do have alot of urinary frequency. I don't have leakage though. I had 100 units of Botox in my bladder 4 days ago. My uro wanted to do 200 units, but I thought starting out with a smaller dose would be better because of so many people talking about retention issues. He also told me that I would notice a difference the next day. Well, I didn't. It is now day 4 and I am no better or worse. I called the uro's office and the nurse said that she would pass along the message to the uro to see if he wanted to add more units. For those of you who have had Botox, can you tell me please how many units you had and when it started working? I've read that it can take a few days to a week or even more, but my uro was sure that I would have noticed a difference by now. I'm thinking I should have let him do the 200 units to begin with. Any info would be appreciated. Thanks! Paula

Ok so, I didn't get any response to my thread about Botox. I went to my urologist's office again last Friday and had an additional 100 units of Botox (200 units total) since the 100 units he did a week prior did nothing. The procedure was ALOT more painful this time around. I was literally screaming and crying. My doctor didn't anything to console me and just left the room after it was over. I don't know if he just didn't know what to say or he didn't know what to do with me crying. Anyways, I have had no relief from my constant urge to urinate. Yesterday and today, I have had to strain alittle bit to urinate and the flow kept stopping and restarting. I hope I'm not going into retention! I don't know if I'm starting to get an infection or not. Like I said, I still have the constant feeling of needing to urinate, but there is no pain that would indicate a possible UTI. Maybe the feeling is from the straining to urinate....? I called the nurse and she prescribed some Cipro for me to take just in case I was getting an infection. I'd rather be safe than sorry. Has anyone else had experience with Botox?

Re: AnYoNe TrIeD BoToX iNjEcTiOnS?? I have not gotten any problems with the Botox. I started with them in 2003 and until know. I get them about every 8 months and get the Botox in the Doctor office under local. The first three time I got it done with general anes.

contents: Somehow I was able to get in to do the botox to the pelvic floor last Thursday. i went during my lunch at work, and it took maybe 15 min? It felt like a giant shot in your vagina. The worst part was them feeling around for the area that hurts the worse. It was kinda scary, but it wasn't that bad tbh. I had no anesthesia, and it was fine. I did the trial for interstim without anesthesia the first round, so what else could be worse than that? It costed me $55 total because I fought hard with my insurance over it. I had to call my drs office multiple times over and over. At first, it was denied. i told them to appeal and they did not listen. So I kept calling my financial person at the hospital and telling them look there's no way I am paying $2k please tell them to appeal. So finally the damn nurse sent in my letter, and guess what, it was accepted. So ridiculous. Then when i went in to get the injuections, my dr claimed it was the nurse who did all the work. Uh no, I spent 3 hrs on the phone just to get her to do her job. They are so arrogant. I hope it works but who knows...:/ I have heard it can take 2 weeks to kick in? Anyone have experience with this? I was barely sore afterwards. It hurt more because I have vulvar vestibulitis. Those with VV should prepare to put on lidocaine after cause it hurts.

contents: So Botox for my PELVIC FLOOR was approved by my insurance. After weeks of telling my doctors office to APPEAL for it to be covered and mark it as MEDICALLY NECESSARY, my insurance finally approved it.My pharmaceutical insurance is Caremark. Tell them to mark it as URGENT and they have 72 hrs to decide and a dr will call your dr and discuss it. My doctors SWORE it would never get approved and "has never seen it approved. I was like yeah right i know how to play this game and called them over and over until they did it and kept following up. I even had to call the financial aid person at my hospital to tell my dr to do it because the price was $2k with the procedure and medication. FINALLY A WIN! Now I am waiting to get a new appointment to do it and see if the procedure will also be covered. The financial aid person said most likely it will be covered if the medication is. I am still not confident it will help my frequency but it's nice to win one.

contents: So, my story is long, but I will just shorten it to that I've had urinary frequency/pelvic pain for over 2 years this month. I've been to a million uros and gynos. I finally found a gyno I like. She thinks it is PFD or vaginismus, and I think it is that and IC. I have already done most procedures that comes with IC from OAB pills to instills to anti depressants to diet changes and even the interstim. Google that. I failed every single one. Check my signature. I tried pelvic floor therapy for 2 months, and the pt got frustrated and told me to stop. My gyno decided it was time to move on to botox. I have had no relief, still going to the bathroom every damn hour, still in pain. i am still trying to dilate at home and have moved up to the second. I also have vulvar vestibulitis because why not, so it hurts more than normal. I use lidocaine during the dilation and have been using gapapentin on the area. TO THE MAIN QUESTION: I just started a new job a few months ago and have already had to go to an appt. I am nervous of asking off again for the botox,but I am dying to try it. It will cost me $600, but idc at this point. (btw I am also seeing a new uro too because the current is dismissive.) The gyno scheduled me to do it at 11:45 in 2 weeks which would work great for me so I can go to lunch and come back. Do you think this is possible with the pain? How much did it hurt during and after? I know I am not being put to sleep for sure. Honestly, I had the "trial" interstim put in without anesthesia, and that hurt so much. But I def could have gone to work after. But this will be in my vagina (duh), and I sit at work allday. ):

contents: Hello all, I have been in a lot of pain and with increased urgency and frequency since my hydrodistention a few weeks ago. Anyways my urologist I saw today has told me that they can do botox injections into my pelvic floor tonight!! It might help because pfd is a player in IC disease. I of course said yes, but I am nervous because it is so last minute! Thankfully my favourite urologist is doing it, as his partner is my usual dr. But I like this one lots better. I was told incontinence is a possibility, also he said he has seen 1 person during his residency develop rectal incontinence. He has never had it happen while he has done it, but still nerve wracking! If people could share what their experiences were to prepare me for what to expect that would be great!

contents: Re: Botox injections today!! Any advice?? My Botox injections have been in my bladder, not into my pelvic floor, so I'm sure it'll be different for you. I will mention that it can take as much as a couple of weeks for the Botox to ramp up to its full effect. Be prepared for an adjustment period of a few days to a couple of weeks while everything settles down; during this time you may have varying symptoms (hopefully gradually decreasing, but not necessarily at a predictable rate) and changing side effects (which my urologist told me were likely to peak at the 2-week point for the bladder injections, then taper off after that). Please let us know how your experience is with Botox! I hope it gives you a lot of relief from your symptoms.

Re: Botox injections today!! Any advice?? Oh god I was crying until I read your post because I woke up with the strong need to pee still so I was just devastated cuz I thought it didn't work! Thank you very much, I am hopeful again, I guess this is what happens when I don't get a chance to do any research on a procedure before having it done haha. I will update you as the days progress, I do really hope this helps though, otherwise I'm doing injections right into the bladder. Risk of Incontinence or retention be damned, I am gona get relief somehow. Thank yoouu 💜💜

contents: Re: Botox injections today!! Any advice?? So this is day 3 after botox. I am noticing I'm a lot more tired than I used to be. I'll spend a few hours on the couch watching TV when I wake up, then drive to my dr. Appointment, and get home by 3 only to like half sleep half zen for 5 hours on the couch with my heating pad. I did this yesterday and today. And my sleeps at night are worse too. I wake up more often and they are more restless. Also the frequency seems to have increased today, it had been holding fairly steady, but today around 4pm I started needing the bathroom every 15 to 20 minutes. I'm not sure if this is an infection, or something that can happen with botox in the pelvic area, but I am slightly worried. Especially because I'm supposed to pack tonight and make a 5 hour drive tomorrow to start university again. At this point I'm legitimately concerned that I will be unable to care for myself or have what it takes to get through this year of nursing.

contents: Re: Botox injections today!! Any advice?? Hi Le, I don't have direct experience myself with botox into pelvic floors but I do know quite a bit about it from years of research for my son who has around 1500 units into his legs for cerebral palsy. Which is a huge amount compared to what I get in my face a mere 11 units:-) Anyway, they warn everyone regarding the incontinence because botox has been known to cause this REGARDLESS of the site of injection. The closer to the bladder the more strongly the side effect. It is rare but they tell you anyway. And remember it is temporary so if anything goes wrong you will be ok . Your body will remove it and you will back to normal again. You probably only had about 100 units in your pelvic floor and it will last anything up to 4-5 months. Mine only lasts a couple of months as does my sons. I think you're doing the right thing by relaxing these last couple of days. If you had botox injections into the pelvic floor I gather you went under general and that is most probably why you're super tired. It can knock you around for a while. Good luck, I would love to hear how you went with it.

contents: Re: Botox injections today!! Any advice?? I had my botox intervention two weeks ago. I felt good about three days... But now i can tell you that it didn't work. My urologist said that it depends of each person, there are people that this treatment works while others not. I wish you the best. Good luck!

I'd posted in another thread, but I have enough questions that I decided it was worth starting my own. My doctor wants to start doing botox injections. She's never done them before, but she's working with the manufacturer's rep to get set up to start. I'm a little worried about being her guinea pig, but at the same time she's an awesome doctor and I'd be very reluctant to switch. My main issues are frequency and urgency. I have some pain that's often bad enough to be very unpleasant, but not quite to the point of being debilitating. I have urge incontinence that comes and goes, so that I usually have a couple of bouts a year that will last anywhere from two weeks to two months. I currently take Elmiron and Vesicare, and occasionally hyoscyamine when the urgency and pain get bad. I try not to take the hyoscyamine because it makes me sleepy, as do a lot of the other meds I've tried. I do regular instillations with heparin, gentamicin, Marcaine, and sodium bicarbonate, mostly daily but sometimes less often when my symptoms quiet down (I do my own instillations, so I can decide on the fly when I need them). I'm also doing PTNS, where I'm about nine weeks into the twelve week treatment, and pelvic floor PT. I was doing great with the PTNS until late October, but in mid-September I got a biliary obstruction and ended up hospitalized in mid-October with ascending cholangitis. I had an ERCP and balloon dilation of some of my bile ducts, but I've continued to have some pain and other biliary obstruction symptoms and may end up getting biliary stents placed. After my hospitalization (three days for the ascending cholangitis, and overnight for a poor reaction to the anesthesia for the ERCP) my bladder symptoms flared up badly, and I've been fighting to get them back under control. It's just in the past couple of days that I've felt like I'm making progress, but the urgency is continues to be a problem. If I go forward with the botox injections, will it calm down the urgency? Will it help the pain? Is the potential hassle of having to catheterize myself multiple times a day worth the improvement? Despite how much I like my urologist, should I look for a doctor who's got more experience with the botox injections? I'm more scared by the botox injection that I've been by other treatments - hydrodistentions, starting instillations, and learning to do my own daily instillations have all worried me some, but I don't think I was as worried about them as much as I am about the botox. Maybe it's because it's less well established as a treatment than the other things I do, maybe it's because I worry that it'll do permanent harm instead of helping, or maybe it's tied up in my general anxiety about my health situation (which is not all that great). I don't know why. But I do know that I'm scared of making my bladder worse.

Re: Starting Botox First of all, I have not tried botox. We have found other treatment options that work well for me so it hasn't been an issue. That said, I have not heard anyone say they had long term negative side effects from botox. Since you already are able to cath yourself, I won't need to mention that. I personally thing it's a good idea for anyone with IC to be able to catheterize, if necessary --- before I learned, I had a couple of ER nightmares, since I live a little more than a half hour from the ER and I was already in pain when we started for there --- then had to wait for treatment. I was my urologist's first IC patient when he went into practice, which in my case turned out to be a real benefit. He really went to bat for me, attended seminars to learn about IC, researched for and with me, and shared his findings with me. At the time I worked in nursing administration in the hospital where he practiced and he actually would stop by my office with copies of articles. The reason I mention this is that, even if your doctor hasn't done botox before, if she's doing her research and learning, she's most likely able to safely do the procedure. I'm always interested in how the various treatments work out --- so please post to let me know what you decide and how it works out for you. Warm healing thoughts, Donna

contents: Re: Starting Botox I am really glad that you have posted this here. I am really hoping to do botox if my current hydrodistention does not work. Can you explain these injections? THe botox treatment I am considering is done the same way as a hydrodistention but they do numerous injections into the bladder wall. Is this the same as you are considering? Thanks for your help.
signature: Current Meds: 1 Elmiron 100mg 3x a day 1 Cimetidine 300mg 2x a day 4 Gabapentin 300mg 3x a day (3600mg) 1 - 2 Atarax 25mg at bedtime 1 baclofen 10mg 3x a day as needed 200mg of Pyridium 3x a day (usually do in cycles - on for a week or two as needed) - I am very careful about this. 2 belladonna and opium suppositories as needed for pain daily. Bladder instillations (Lidocaine, Heparin, & Sodium Bicarbonate) I have tried but failed - lidocaine instillations, heparin instillations, oxybutynin by mouth also called ditropan, tylenol 3s with coedine, cyclobenzaphrine, Ativan, vesicare, Oxytrol patches worked for 3 to 4 years and then failed, I tried Uracyst and had some success but my urologist felt that it was not successful so we moved on to trying something else. . [/I]

contents: Re: Starting Botox Yes, that's the one I'm considering. As I understand it, the Botox spreads from the injection sites throughout the bladder wall, which basically weakens the muscles. This, in turn, reduces the spasms that cause pain and urgency. There are many injection sites so that the Botox will spread evenly. I assume it's similar to a cystoscopy, where they put enough water in the bladder to give them room to see and work, but not as much as a hydrodistention where they're actually stretching the bladder. It's often done as an office procedure with no anesthesia, so it hopefully isn't too painful.

Re: Starting Botox Originally posted by ltapilot Yes, that's the one I'm considering. As I understand it, the Botox spreads from the injection sites throughout the bladder wall, which basically weakens the muscles. This, in turn, reduces the spasms that cause pain and urgency. There are many injection sites so that the Botox will spread evenly. I assume it's similar to a cystoscopy, where they put enough water in the bladder to give them room to see and work, but not as much as a hydrodistention where they're actually stretching the bladder. It's often done as an office procedure with no anesthesia, so it hopefully isn't too painful. That sounds better - to be done in office. I wish you every success.

contents: Re: Starting Botox Originally posted by wizbe That sounds better - to be done in office. I wish you every success. The past two times I've had general anesthesia for an outpatient procedure, I've ended up hospitalized overnight, so I like that it's an office procedure. My doctor warned me that the cystoscope can be uncomfortable, but I'd rather put up with some discomfort and get to go home afterwards. I don't have the procedure scheduled yet. My recent flare has mostly quieted down, so there's not as much of a rush to get the Botox. I think I'll still go through with it, though, in hopes that it'll calm the ongoing urgency that's persisted despite my flare getting better. I'll post when I've had it and let folks know how it went.

Re: Starting Botox One thing that can help with office procedure is to relax. You might want to practice some techniques to help you relax. What I do is to concentrate on breathing slowly and deeply --- and consciously relax my muscles. That works for me for catheters and also for blood draws. I have monthly blood draws and I can stay relaxed completely while the needle is inserted. I'll watch for your report. Donna

contents: Re: Starting Botox Do your own research on Botox. I became allergic to Botox injections injected into facial lines and it took almost eight months of horrible sides to subside. Remember, it is a toxin!

Re: Starting Botox I talked to my urologist. Apparently all I'm waiting on is a prior authorization from my insurance. She wants to get it done next week, but there's no telling how long my insurance people will hold it up. lpsievert, as you suggested I did some reading. It appears that allergy is a rare effect, usually to one of the other ingredients in the injection. Could you tell me more? I'd like to hear about your experience.

Re: Starting Botox I developed side effects from facial injections of botox after 7 plus years of getting injections with no problems. I think my experience is really rare, but I did develop side effects 10 days after injection, including migraine headache, swelling in forehead, nausea, tightness in chest, weakness, wheezing and drooping upper eyelid. I had migraines that lasted 8 months, but I think the botox effected my trigeminal nerve. It might have been to much botox injected or placement of injection, I'll never know. Ask your urologist how many times he has done these injections and if he has had any patients who experienced adverse reactions. I'm sure you'll be fine, good luck to you!

Re: Starting Botox I have my appointment this afternoon at 3:30. I'm hoping that I'll feel OK tonight - my wife and I have had tickets for the new Star Wars movie for two months (along with a bunch of geeky friends - 23 total people in our group), so it's frustrating that the appointment ended up on the same day. If I'm not feeling up to going, though, I'll send my oldest son instead and catch the movie another time. Going to a premier is fun, but getting relief from the pain and urgency will be more fun. :-) I will post after the procedure to let everyone know how it went, and probably follow up in a few weeks just to update how the treatment has worked. Thank you, everyone, for your thoughts and prayers. They mean more to me than I can put into words.

Re: Starting Botox Well, it's done. She put lidocaine gel in my urethra, and lidocaine liquid in my bladder. The procedure was very tolerable, although it was getting uncomfortable toward the end because she was having to really work with the scope to get the injections in the right place. She said a rigid scope would be easier, and I think I'll ask for that next time. I'm pretty sore now, three hours later. I have urethral burning and continual leakage of urine that I've experienced with other cystoscopies. It's getting better, perhaps because I've taken pyridine and Dilautid. :-) It will take a few days to a couple of weeks to find out if it's helping or not, and whether I am going to have retention problems. Trading retention for the incontinence I've been having sounds liveable, if it comes to that. Sorry if this is discombobulated - I'm on some powerful meds!

Re: Starting Botox It's been a day now, and I'm still having a lot of pain in my urethra. The cystoscopy took about 40 minutes, and my urethra isn't happy about that. It's still swollen visibly where I can see near the opening, and when I pee it feels like slivers of glass coming out. I think the urethral pain is what's contributing to ongoing bladder spasms, which are very painful and are continuing to cause incontinence. I've completely soaked three adult diapers today, which is more leakage than I've had before. I also have the sensation of incomplete emptying of my bladder, but I cathed (with LOTS of lidocaine gel!) and I'm not actually retaining urine. However, I remain hopeful that this will help. The symptoms I'm having now are probably because of the long and complex cystoscopy, not because of the Botox injections. If it does help, it'll be so worth it. Getting rid of the pain, pressure, urgency, and frequency will absolutely be worth a couple of days of urethral discomfort. I can't wait to see how I do as the cystoscopy side effects wane and the Botox effects ramp up.

Re: Starting Botox Originally posted by ltapilot my wife and I have had tickets for the new Star Wars movie for two months I also had tickets for a long time for me, my wife and two daughters for the opening. (It was the 16th here in Norway). I missed it...too much discomfort the past week to even consider going. But as for your procedure - I thought botox made it likely that you'd need to self-catheterize afterwards? Simply because the bladder muscles no longer function normally due to the injections. I had a cysto three weeks ago and was sore for days afterwards. Anyway congrats on having had the procedure! I hope it works out well for you.
signature: Male (45) living in Oslo, Norway. Diagnosed (cysto) with BPS/IC with Hunner's lesions on 23 Nov 2015. Symptoms: severe bladder pain, bladder cramps, urethral burning, painful urination, rectal spasms, acute retention (requiring self catheterization). Treatment: successful hydro w/fulguration on 21 Dec 2015. Complications due to blood clots in bladder, second surgery cleaned it out. Currently on weekly Uracyst instillations. Almost symptom free! Other diseases: IBS and chronic asthma, both of which are under control through meds and diet.

contents: Re: Starting Botox Retention is the big concern, but I'm not having any trouble with that, at least not yet. It can hit two weeks or more after the procedure. Something like ten percent of patients have to cath at least once after the procedure. I called the on-call urologist today, and he said that my level of pain is not unusual after a long cysto. If he's not worried, I'll take some pain meds, grit my teeth, and carry on.

Re: Starting Botox I am anxious to hear how your recovery goes. I am scheduled for the procedure on Jan. 28th. I, like you have urgency but also a good amount of bladder pain. Between the PF pain and the bladder pain I'm pretty miserable. I will have my procedure in the hospital. Unlike you I have never had to catch myself. Frankly it scares me. I will be looking for your progress. Feel well.

Re: Starting Botox I've gradually been improving this week. My severe urgency and incontinence have subsided, my bladder has settled down and isn't hurting like before, and the urethral pain is still there but much improved. I still haven't some discomfort in the outer 2" or so of my urethra, where it feels like perhaps the doctor should have lubricated the cystoscopy just a but more, but it's much, much better. I'm having some retention issues, but not bad enough to require a catheter. Sometimes I have 60 to 100 ml of urine left, which is not enough to worry about just yet. I have catheters handy and can use them if I need, but so far just sitting down and working on relaxing my pelvic floor is enough. Cathing seems more scary than it really is. It very quickly becomes routine, and it's quick and easy to do. As for the Botox, the jury is still out. The past week has not been very pleasant, and the first few days were downright miserable. Some of that was just the luck of the frame, but had ng a cysto and twenty needle punctures in your bladder is never going to be much fun. My urgency is better than before the Botox, but not as good as I'd like. The Botox needs a little more time to work its magic, so I'm working hard to stay positive and hopeful.

Re: Starting Botox Itapilot, I'm glad to hear you are a little better. I go in for a instillation on Dec. 30. Will have to talk with my doctor a little more about the Botox. If I decide to go forward I think I need to learn how to self cath first. My doctor suggested Botox because I am only getting a could of months relief from hydrodistentions. The first one he did helped the most but he lasered off my largest Hunner's lesions, that helped a lot. Thank you for the quick reply.

Re: Starting Botox Instillations have done wonders for me. I hope they work as well for you. They haven't gotten me completely to the point that I'd like to be (if they had I wouldn't be doing the Botox!) but they've kept things at a point that I can continue working, and that's a big deal for me. Definitely talk to your doc about self cathing when you go for your instillation. It's a very simple procedure. The first few times you'll feel like you're all thumbs, but it quickly gets to be a routine. There are all sorts of online resources to show you how, so you can go to your appointment with a very good idea of how it's going to go and get the most benefit of what the doctor shows you. I hope you get feeling better!

Re: Starting Botox Just a quick update: The Botox finally seems to be taking effect. Today was the first day in several weeks that I haven't done an instillation and haven't had bladder pain. I was out running errands for most of the afternoon, and the urgency and frequency were not even on my radar. I felt normal for the first time on months. I did end up cathing this evening, and I was retaining about 100 ml of urine. Not too bad overall, and as long as it stays this way I'll be pretty happy.

Re: Starting Botox Originally posted by ltapilot Just a quick update: The Botox finally seems to be taking effect. Today was the first day in several weeks that I haven't done an instillation and haven't had bladder pain. I was out running errands for most of the afternoon, and the urgency and frequency were not even on my radar. I felt normal for the first time on months. I did end up cathing this evening, and I was retaining about 100 ml of urine. Not too bad overall, and as long as it stays this way I'll be pretty happy. That's great! Thank you for sharing. Donna

contents: Re: Starting Botox Good time for another update. It's been 13 days now since I had the Botox injections, and things are continuing to change but at a slower rate. I have had almost no pain, and I've only done two instillation since the morning of the 24th. However, the retention volume has steadily increased - on 12/24 it was 100 ml, on 12/27 it was 140 ml, and yesterday it was 200 ml. I can sometimes sit a while and relax and get a bit more out, but it's still enough to be a problem, so I've been cathing three to five times a day. That's the bad part. The good part is that my incontinence is gone. My last significant accident was on the 20th during the recovery from the cystoscopy, and I haven't worn a diaper since the 22nd. I'm somewhat at a loss for words to describe how happy that makes me. The biggest hassle of using catheters regularly is figuring out how to carry them discreetly at work and such. I've been tucking them in my jacket pocket and pulling them out as I need them, then tucking them into my pants pocket before going into the restroom. Works for now, but if I'm still having retention issues when the weather warms up I'll have to find someplace other than my jacket to keep them. Overall, I'm thrilled with the results. If the Botox continues to work as well as it has been, I'll just about be able to forget that I have IC. I've continued to be careful about what I eat and drink, and I suspect I'll have to continue to do that, but my symptoms are so much better that there's not really even a comparison. I'll wait to see how it does over time before I make a final judgement, but it's looking pretty good for the moment.

Re: Starting Botox That is encouraging to hear. Although the retention is of concern. That is the one thing that concerns me the most about having it done. My reason for considering the Botox is solely based on my pain levels. I do not have any incontitence at all. I do have urgency as my bladder fills and becomes very painful if I cannot go to the restroom immediately. I have an appointment tomorrow for an instillation. I will talk to my doctor about learning how to self cath before the procedure on Jan. 28. Did your doctor tell you how long you may need to self cath? I'm so pleased to hear you are finding relief. I truly hope it continues for a long time.

Re: Starting Botox I am glad you pain levels are down. The urologist I recently saw offered botox. I did not feel comfortable due to the high rate of retention issues ( I do Not want to cath as it causes UTI's and I am very prone to them and am allergic to most all antibiotics). I also am very chemical sensitive and my allergist was not to fond of botox being injected into my bladder. She said if I were to have an allergic reaction it would be rough because there is no way to get the botox out of my body, I would have to wait months for it to wear off. To much risk for me. I am glad you are feeling better in some ways, but sorry you are dealing with the retention issues. I hope that calms down soon.

Re: Starting Botox Retention is the main issue that many people have. I've had retention a couple of times previously after hydrodistentions and due to painkillers when I've been hospitalized, so I seem to be prone to it. So it didn't surprise me that I was having it, although I'm surprised and happy that it's not complete retention where I can't get anything out without using a catheter. I didn't discuss the possible length of time I'll be cathing with my doctor because I'd read quite a bit about it already, and the short answer is that there is no telling. During clinical trials, among the 6.5% of patients who had retention and had to start self cathing, the minimum duration was one day and the maximum was 214 days, with a median of 63 days. So of people who get botox (100 units, which is what I got - MS patients and other neurogenic bladder problems get a higher dose), about one in eighteen need to start self cathing, and at 63 days half of those were continuing to self cath. I can understand not wanting to do the botox because of allergy issues or chemical sensitivities. If it's just the issue of retention and possibly having to use a catheter, though, I wouldn't worry too much - 17 of every 18 people who get botox at the dosage that's likely to be used for IC don't end up needing to self cath, and even for those who do it's something that resolves in two months or less for half of them. I'm also learning that it's not all that big a deal - it takes me five minutes in the restroom instead of one minute, and I have to wash my hands before I start as well as after I finish, but the total time I'm spending in the restroom now is less than when my IC is flaring and I'm in there 20+ times a day. Last night I got thinking about getting a hard case for eyeglasses to put a couple of catheters and a tube of Lidocaine jelly in - then I can be very discreet about carrying my supplies, and I don't have to worry that the lube will leak and make a huge mess in my pocket (which happened the other day - sticking your hand into a jeans pocket full of KY jelly is quite the experience!) As I said above, I'm happy with the results I'm getting. I'm not hurting, I'm using the bathroom six or seven times a day, and I'm not having accidents. Life seems pretty good right at the moment!

Re: Starting Botox I saw my doctor today for an instillation. We talked about my up coming Botox injections. He said he will us between 200-300 because of the condition if my bladder. They will teach me how to cath before I leave the hospital. I am encouraged by your progress. I don't look forward to having to self cath but I guess I will get over it and maybe I will be one of the lucky ones and not have a retention problem. I asked if there is any way we can test and see if I am allergic to the Botox, something I had not even thought of. He said there really wasn't unless I wanted to order Botox to test before, but the insurance would not pay for it and it runs about $700! I hope you continue to improve. I will be interested how long you need to cath and how long the Botox continues to help you.

Re: Starting Botox I am so happy to hear your encouraging results! I wish you continued success. I am having botox injections under general anesthesia on Monday. I am excited and nervous all wrapped up in one. I am hopeful with botox as I feel that's the way I am supposed to go (it just feels right for me!). I need to be able to work and right now, as a counselor it is rather difficult when I need to pee anywhere from 4 to 5 times an hour. I have never been this bad and I have had this condition for 8 years. I am having 100 units of botox as well. Most research documented has been using 100 units for interstitial cystitis (the research I have read anyway). I will let you all know how it goes. Many blessings :-)

contents: Re: Starting Botox Wizbe, I will be thinking of you on Monday. I hope the Botox will provide you with the relief you are looking for. My procedure is not until the end of January. My doctor said he will inject between 200-300 units. While I have frequency issues my greatest problem is pain. I have had IC with Hunner's Lesions for 9 years. I was fortunate enough to be at an age I could take early retirement, I could not have stayed in a classroom for another 5 years. Good luck. Will be following your progress.

Re: Starting Botox hi signgal. Thank you. I understand not being in the classroom anymore. I am a school counsellor and I often teach in classrooms. I haven't been able to do that. I wish you every success. I will write how I do with the treatment later.
signature: Current Meds: 1 Elmiron 100mg 3x a day 1 Cimetidine 300mg 2x a day 4 Gabapentin 300mg 3x a day (3600mg) 1 - 2 Atarax 25mg at bedtime 1 baclofen 10mg 3x a day as needed 200mg of Pyridium 3x a day (usually do in cycles - on for a week or two as needed) - I am very careful about this. 2 belladonna and opium suppositories as needed for pain daily. contents: Re: Starting Botox Wizbe, I hope your injection procedure is going well. You're in my prayers. Signgal, that's a lot of Botox! 200 is what they use for multiple sclerosis patients, and something like 30% of patients have retention afterwards. If your doctor's experienced, though, I suppose he'll have his reasons. I continue to feel good. I've been routinely measuring voided volume, and my retained volume is typically 200 to 250 ml, which is only slightly more than five days ago, and it's not increasing anymore. Given that I can still void some without a catheter, I think I'm probably going to skip the problem of complete retention. There's enough left in my bladder if I don't use a catheter that I feel immediately like I have to pee again, but at least if I'm away from home and have forgotten to bring a catheter, I can avoid overdistending my bladder. I need to be careful of that, though - yesterday morning I voided 550 ml including what I drained with the catheter, and that's generally considered to be too much. If I keep having an overfilled bladder in the morning, I may need to start setting an alarm to get me up in the middle of the night to empty it. I did have a somewhat alarming episode Saturday. I was doing an instillation because I was having some bladder pain (it's only the third instill I've done since the procedure on the 17th, so I'm doing them once a week, more or less - way less than the daily instills I was doing before the Botox). When I took the catheter out, I started bleeding. It was probably 15-20 drops of blood, but it looked like a lot - I was sitting on the edge of my bed at the time and it made a big puddle on the floor, then left drips all the way to the bathroom. It stopped within five minutes or so, but it was scary when it was going on. I looked it up online and it seemed that it probably wasn't a big deal (they say "slight" bleeding is normal, but "heavy" bleeding may require an ER visit - but no definition of how much is "slight" or "heavy.") Since it stopped and wasn't hurting, I waited and talked to my urologist's nurse this morning. The nurse said she'd mention it to the doctor, and I see the doctor on Thursday, so I'm betting they'll take a look then, and it's likely not any big cause for concern, but it was scary since I didn't know what was going on or whether it was really serious. Overall, things are good, and I'm happy with the results. The catheters are a minor inconvenience compared with the pain and urgency that they replaced. I don't know how long it'll last, but typical time seems to be in the 8-10 month range. If I can feel as good as I do now for that long, I'll go have it done again when it wears off. I'm not quite ready to call it a complete success - I had at one point thought I'd found my magic bullet with hydrodistension (which quit helping much after about three rounds) and instillations (which helped a lot, but had to be done daily to keep the good results). But I'm enjoying the results now, and I'm trying not to worry too much about what the future holds. I see my urologist later this week, and I'll probably post another update based on that.

Re: Starting Botox I had my urologist followup today, which is the first time I've seen my doctor since my Botox injections. The appointment was easy, consisting just of discussing symptoms, answering questions, and talking about schedules. I have a followup scheduled for four months after the procedure, but I have my doctor's blessing to move it up or back depending on how I'm feeling. What I told the doctor is that I'm overall very satisfied with the Botox. It's not been perfect, but it's far, far better than what I've been dealing with for the last couple of years. I can void without a catheter, but I am still retaining around 200 ml of urine after I finish voiding so I'm cathing after every void. She pointed out that this normally gets better, and while I'll look forward to that, it's just not that big a deal anymore. I remember early in my diagnosis process I got a VCUG, which involved filling my bladder with X-ray absorbing fluid and watching via fluoroscope while I voided, and the worst and scariest part was getting the catheter put in. Now I'm cathing myself seven or eight times a day, and it's quick, easy, and painless. I also told her that I still have some odd urgency problems. Generally, in the late morning and most of the afternoon, I'll feel like I need to pee, but if I get up and go the volume is low. If I ignore it for a while, it goes away, and I can wait until I feel the pressure of my bladder being full to go use the bathroom. She thinks that may be bladder irritation from the catheter, and suggested that I instill 5ml of Marcaine and 5ml of sodium bicarbonate after I cath to see if that will help. I will try this, and hopefully it'll help. I also mentioned that I don't get much of a sensation of when my bladder is full, and it's mostly just a slowly increasing feeling of pressure. If I'm not paying attention, I can go until my bladder is very distended, to the point that I've gotten as much as 600 ml out. This did not seem to be very concerning to the doctor, so maybe I'm making too much of a deal out of it. The best solution is just to get used to how my bladder feels when I do need to drain it, and how it feels when I don't really need to drain it. Overall, I'm very happy with where things are now. It's been a long road to get here, and there have been other things that I thought would be the magic bullet that weren't, but at least for the moment I'm very hopeful that my pain and urgency are going to be much, much better for a while, and that the Botox will continue to work for me.

Re: Starting Botox Itapilot, it is nice to hear you are doing so well. I certainly understand about hoping for that "magic bullet," most of us have tried various treatments hoping for relief. Some things may help for a time or work for one person and not others. I truly hope I live long enough to see a cure or at least a treatment that will allow us to live a normal life again. I have such mixed feelings about having this done, I'm always fearful that I might come out worse. Thank you for keeping us posted about your journey.

Re: Starting Botox It's been a bit of a rough week. I started having upper abdominal pain on Wednesday, and ended up hospitalized from Saturday evening until Monday evening for it. They don't actually know what's causing it (it's still ongoing), but they think it may be peptic ulcer disease. One interesting thing that surprised me about being in the hospital is that they were TERRIBLE about having catheter supplies. With all the IV fluids, my bladder was filling every hour and a half to two hours, and once I ran out of the supplies I'd brought from home it was a bit of a battle to find appropriate stuff. They label every patient as a fall risk and don't want you to get out of bed without someone to help you, and they didn't have waterless hand sanitizer besides in the wall-mounted dispensers that I couldn't reach, so unless I waited for the tech every time I needed to go I couldn't clean my hands. They told me not to have my wife bring in more catheters from home, since they'd supply what I needed, but they what they kept bringing me various types of Foley catheters. They brought me regular Foleys and double-lumen Foleys in both silicone-coated latex and in silicone. all of which will work but none of which is really ideal. They only had sterile lubricant in tiny little paper packages that I couldn't insert into my urethra like I usually do with my own lube, and the viscosity was thick enough that it was hard to get it to transfer from the packet to the catheter. Plus, it seemed like every time I'd try to cath, someone else would walk into the room - they'd knock as they were walking in, not even giving me enough time to get covered back up. I was routinely getting more than 500 ml out of my bladder, and on one occasion had 800 ml and another time 1000 ml, both of those accompanied by lots of pain and a complete inability to pee even enough to take the pressure off. The whole experience was uncomfortable, and surprising considering how often hospitals have to deal with catheters. I'm back at work now, still having the abdominal pain and hoping my gastroenterologist's office will call soon to schedule a followup appointment. Next time I'm in the hospital, I suppose I'll either bring in enough catheters to get me through the stay, or just ask for a Foley.

Click on ICN Shop at the top of this page. You'll find Bladder Builder and Bladder Rest, both of which we are finding have excellent results. contents: Re: Starting Botox Itapilot, how awful for you. Being in the hospital for any reason is stressful and being concerned about your need to self cath must have made it even more so. I'm surprised they didn't place a foley cath. I hope they will find the cause of your pain quickly. Do you think it has anything to do with your recent Botox? Be well.

Re: Starting Botox I've been continuing to have abdominal pain since I got out of the hospital, but I don't think it's related to my botox injections. I have ulcerative colitis (bowel inflammation) and primary sclerosing cholangitis (bile duct inflammation), so I'm thinking the pain is related to one of those. It could also be a gastric ulcer, despite the fact that they didn't see one when they did the endoscopy. I've been having a frustrating time trying to get in to see my GI - the hospital told me to follow up in two weeks, and his office finally got me scheduled yesterday for an appointment in three more weeks (four weeks from my hospitalization). I pointed out that it's continuing to hurt, and I think the GI's response is going to be to write me a prescription for more pain meds rather than finding an opening in the schedule when he can see me sooner. My retention has been stable for a couple of weeks, typically running 200 to 250 ml, but occasionally more or less. I had a fairly major bleeding episode on Sunday. I did a marcaine instillation in the morning, and the next time I cathed was six hours later. I didn't think about it, and the marcaine numbs my bladder to the point that I can really hold quite a bit. When I did pee, I got out 700 ml of urine with so much blood in it that it looked brown. Lots and lots of dark red blood. I called my insurance company's triage nurse, who suggested I get seen for it on Monday. The next time I had to pee, I was nervous, but what came out was the usual pale yellow. However, every time I've cathed since then, there's been blood on the end of the catheter. I gave my urologist a sample yesterday, and their quick test said that there was some blood in it but no infection. They'll send it to the lab for a culture to make sure. My urologist wasn't concerned about it, eve with the description of how much blood was in there the first time, so I suppose I shouldn't panic. It's hard to see lots of my own blood and not get nervous, though. :-) I also got a letter from my insurance company telling me that my current urologist could no longer be my specialist, and I'd have to pick somebody else. It turns out that they sent the paperwork for renewing her acceptance of my insurance to the wrong address, so she never got it. I'm kind of peeved at my insurance company, though, since I spent the whole weekend stressing about finding another doctor who is willing to work with me on my IC issues instead of just shrugging it off and leaving me to suffer. It sounds like it's getting sorted out, though, so I don't have to change doctors after all. Other than minor issues with sometimes bleeding because of the catheters, the botox continues to be wonderful. I am going to the restroom six to eight times a day, and my bladder isn't hurting. I don't have a lot of sensation of when I do have to pee, so I have to pay attention to avoid getting my bladder overfilled, but I'm getting the schedule down so that it's not going to be a problem. As long as I keep feeling like this, life is good.

Re: Starting Botox Hello Wizbe, I wanted to check in with you and see how you are doing since your Botox injections. I'm scheduled for next week. I would appreciate it if you felt comfortable sharing your experience and outcome. My main reason for trying this is because of bladder and pelvic pain.
Re: Starting Botox Hi signgal. You can read all about my experience in the thread, Botox - My Experience.
signature: Current Meds: 1 Elmiron 100mg 3x a day 1 Cimetidine 300mg 2x a day 4 Gabapentin 300mg 3x a day (3600mg) 1 - 2 Atarax 25mg at bedtime 1 baclofen 10mg 3x a day as needed 200mg of Pyridium 3x a day (usually do in cycles - on for a week or two as needed) - I am very careful about this. 2 belladonna and opium suppositories as needed for pain daily. Bladder instillations (Lidocaine, Heparin, & Sodium Bicarbonate) I have tried but failed - lidocaine instillations, heparin instillations, oxybutynin by mouth also called ditropan, tylenol 3s with coedine, cyclobenzaphrine, Ativan, vesicare, Oxytrol patches worked for 3 to 4 years and then failed, I tried Uracyst and had some success but my urologist felt that it was not successful so we moved on to trying something else. . [/I]

contents: Re: Starting Botox Good time for another update. Not much has changed. The bleeding quit and hasn't come back, so I'm happy about that. I'm not getting irritation from the catheters most of the time, and when I do it subsides in a few minutes. I'm still routinely retaining 250 ml - the amount has settled down into that, instead of bouncing around, but it's still enough residual volume that I have to cath. I don't know how long I'll have to catheterize, but I don't care all that much - my insurance company finally came through with the order from the medical supply, and last week a box with 200 catheters arrived. That should get me through about a month (maybe a little less, but it's the maximum the insurance will cover per month so I may end up buying one box a month out of pocket). It's become such a routine thing now that I hardly have to think about it, and it's rarely even slightly uncomfortable. The only remaining hassle is that a trip to the bathroom takes me three minutes now instead of one, since I have to wash my hands first, pee, cath, tuck the catheter away in my pocket for later disposal, and wash my hands again. Small potatoes. The frequency and pain are still gone. I'm cathing 7 times most days, sometimes as few as 6 and sometimes as much as 8, so I'm visiting the restroom at the same frequency as everyone else. And I'm not hurting in between. I still have a bit of urgency if I go too long before cathing, and I have to pay attention since I don't get much sensation that I need to pee before the urgency comes, but I haven't had a single instance of incontinence even with the urgency, and if I pay attention I don't even get the urgency. Now if I can just solve my bowel pain issues, I'll be set. As you may recall, I was hospitalized a couple of weeks ago with upper abdominal pain, and had an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD). Today I had a colonoscopy. Neither has definitely found the source of my pain, which continues to be moderate. Last night, after the colonoscopy prep, it was bad enough that it took me hours to go to sleep. Today it's bad enough to keep me from being very productive at work (and the meds they gave me for the colonoscopy this morning aren't helping that either). I'm so tired of hurting - just when I get my bladder pain under control, my bowel pain has to ramp up. I've been taking Tramadol for the pain when it's not too bad, and Vicodin when it's bad, but I'd like to be off the pain killers. We'll see how I can accomplish that - I'll keep pushing on the issue until it's resolved. Maybe I need some Botox in my bowels as well. ;-)

Re: Starting Botox Itapilot, it is discouraging that you are still having problems a month and a half after your procedure. I was hoping at this point you woul be saying you had never felt better. My procedure has been moved from last Thursday to this coming Friday. This past month has been a struggle. I'm living off of Tramadol, pyridium and Valium suppositories. This Botox procedure is one of desperation. Hope I don't regret it.
Re: Starting Botox Donna, what has helped you manage your IC. I have terrible bladder pain ( Hunner's lesions), burning and PFD. Botox is my next step, which scares me silly.

Re: Starting Botox Signal, all of my problems relate to my gastrointestinal and liver issues. My IC is better than it has been for many years, with the slight inconvenience (and that's really all it is) of having to use catheters. Having the Botox done was a very good choice for me, and I hope yours will go just as well. I will continue to pray that you get the relief you need from your IC.

Re: Starting Botox Botox day is finally here. I have tried to keep it a secret from my bladder but "someone" spilled the beans because it is very unhappy today. I will try to post tomorrow. Thank you Itapilot & Wizbe for sharing your experiences and giving me encouragement to move forward with this procedure.
Re: Starting Botox Best wishes signgal. I hope it is everything you need it to be.

contents: Re: Starting Botox I hope you're holding in there, signal! The first few days can be rough, but it gets better.

Re: Starting Botox Botox, day 4 since procedure. I'm feeling well. I have not taken anything for pain the last two days. Went to breakfast with my husband this morning then a little shopping. I was wiped out by the time we got back home but felt fine. No bladder pain..let me say that again, no bladder pain. It has been so long since I have been able to say those words. I have had no problem emptying my bladder. Hoping it doesn't begin as I am farther from the procedure. Itapilot, how did your trip to Florida go? Wizbe, are you still pleased with your results? If we get 6-9 months, as claimed, I will be very happy for each of us.

Re: Starting Botox So glad to hear this! I am glad you had NO bladder pain. WOW! That's awesome! Let us know how it continues for you. I have had increased pain for the last four days BUT I still don't feel like I have to pee all the time and I don't have bladder spasms so I like that very much. I am hoping that this morning the pain continues to settle down. The pain is also not near where it has been . . . so that's good. Two instillations in the last two days has brought the pain down - before the botox, bladder instillations wouldn't even touch my pain levels. So this is good :-) I hope it last for all of us for 6 to 9 months! Praying for each of us! Wishing you a wonderful Sunday :-)
signature: Current Meds: 1 Elmiron 100mg 3x a day 1 Cimetidine 300mg 2x a day 4 Gabapentin 300mg 3x a day (3600mg) 1 - 2 Atarax 25mg at bedtime 1 baclofen 10mg 3x a day as needed 200mg of Pyridium 3x a day (usually do in cycles - on for a week or two as needed) - I am very careful about this. 2 belladonna and opium suppositories as needed for pain daily.
