contents: Well I guess it wasn't anything bad because after four days I'm still waiting for a call back. I sent my doctor an email and left a message with the Urology nurse. Luckily things are calming down but I still have pain here and there for reasons unknown. I'm not too surprised because its Kaiser Permanente and they really do not care about their patients.

Can somone please explain or tell me where I can find information on trigger point injections? What's in the injection? What is it for? Who does it? Whare in the body do you receive the injections? Thanks so much!
signature: Laser cervical surgery after abnormal pap (moderate dysplasia) in 3/2004 UTIs since mid 2004 Recurrent yeast infections since mid 2005 IC dx 3/2006 V V dx 12/2006 Other conditions: IBS, allergies, dry eyes. Current IC medications: Elavil (since 12/06) 10 mg; Cysta Q (since 5/07) Rephresh acid jel every 3 days D-mannose, fish oil, Calcium Citrate, Multivitamins & garlic pills (sometimes) Probiotics Femdophilus or other brands Gy-Na-Tren for yeast infections (started using it as needed since 2010) Other Rx:Omeprazole 20mg; benadryl as needed, Restasis Past Meds and why I stopped them: Elmiron: March to September 2006: headaches Lyrica:2 weeks in 2006: headaches Cystoprotek: from Jan to April 2007: nausea Femcon Fe (2/07 to 10/07):caused spotting/bleeding BCP:Loestrin (since Ocotber 2007) to stop spotting for 2 months. V V worse. Stopped the pill in Nov 07. "Obstacles don't have to stop you. If you run into a wall, don't turn around and give up. Figure out how to climb it, go through it, or work around it"

contents: One of my IC/pelvic pain doctors gave me trigger point injections in December. It was NOT Botox. It was a mix of lidocaine and sterioid. It was supposed to help with the internal muscles in spasm causing pain. He used a speculum and a long needle to do them internally. It was not painful when he did them. A little poke... This treatment did not help me. It caused my IC/pelvic pain to flare and my pain level has been high since. That is not to say it couldn't help you. He has a few patients who benefit from it. Good luck.
signature: 24 year old female Diagnosed age 18 with IC conditions: IC, IBS-C, PFD, TMJ, a-typical facial pain Currently taking: -2 Elmiron 100mg capsules 2x a day Pelvic Floor Therapy 1x per month. Acupuncture 2x per week starting 1/18/10.

contents: Hi, I am currently under-going trigger point injections every 3-4 weeks. And I would have to say it has helped me in regards to my pelvic floor dysfunction. It has made it alot easier to void without feeling like I can't go. I use to struggle ALOT with the first void in the morning because I would have such a full bladder & not be able to go but a few drops at a time & weal stream, leaving me to have to sit, stand, sit stand etc...I also did not empty all the way leaving me to have to go more often. I also have horrible spasms that come after voiding. I have a really bad case of IC and it's been tough trying to find something to help. Well since I started these injections, it has helped in this because I can empty better & my spasms are less. The only downside is the injection wears off and it really hasn't helped the internal burning I had in my bladder. I am thinking that that is pain I am just going to have to live with. But I just decided that I kind of have to treat the whole "picture." instead of just one issue. Also the day of the procedure, I am like in ALOT of pain, I don't know if it's from the medicine soaking in & it just has to settle, but its rough. But the next day I do notice a difference. For me, it has been a huge help. Basically from my own personal experience & from what I read they can be injected into the pelvic floor or even in the abdomen. My dr. said that they usually do the injections into the abdomen when you can kind of pinpoint where the pain is, but if its all over its kind of hard. So that is why i'm having them done vaginally on the outside. I am not going to lie, the needle stick is quite painful & has to be injected slowly, so it burns going in. First time, I thought I was going to pass out, but I guess when you've been through alot of treatments like me, it becomes no big deal when your thinking in terms of just trying to get your life back. I figure anything relief is better than nothing. As far as what is used in the injections, it really depends on your dr in what they use. Mine are sodium bi carb, a longer acting lidocaine & a steroid(decadron) I hope this helps

Does anyone know a doctor who will do them in NJ. They all say they will but then when I get there they want to do a years worth of meds first...Meanwhile I am freaking dying and my PT really feels that they may help....They may not but what do I have to loose.
How far from queens are you?
signature: Jenn IC, endometriosis, vulvodynia, PFD, kidney stones, migraines, chronic uti's, ovarian cysts, seizures. Meds: Elmiron 300mg a day a day) Singulair 10mg a day Seasonale Prevacid OTC Xanax-helps pain at times. too many pills for my age!!! "If it wasn't this it would be something else" "There's a happiness to be gained from being able to accept that there are some things that cannot be changed."

contents: I am like Danielle, I had no success with these. The doctor didn't wait until I was sedated enough and it hurt when the needles went in. The next day the pain was so bad I couldn't get out of bed and needed to go to ER. The Dr. who did them would give me nothing for pain only a muscle relaxant and told me I was no longer a candidate (duh). I hurt for so long from those - it was lidocaine and a steriod - I would NEVER do them again. I don't mean to scare anyone and glad some are having good results, but mine was a terrible experience.
signature: Teresa We are not HUMAN BEINGS going through a temporary SPIRITUAL EXPERIENCE. We are SPIRITUAL BEINGS going through a temporary HUMAN EXPERIENCE....so that we may become more SPIRITUAL.

contents: On Thursday morning I went in and got my bladder expanded for the third time and received Botox. This was the first time I had Botox put into my bladder. Everything went fine; I was able to pee like twenty times after the surgery to get the blood clots out and to get down to 150cc to go home. The last two days I feel that I am not emptying my bladder completely. I should be going a lot more after longer periods of time. It’s not like I can’t go at all I just go for a little bit and then it stops. I wait a couple minutes and the urge comes back and I go a little more. I could do this like four times in one sitting. Once I go a couple of times I can start feeling the pain inside my bladder so I may be getting it almost all out. I was hoping that those who have done this and were able to urinate afterwards could tell me if this is normal or if I need to be emptying my bladder every time. My doctor didn’t give me any instructions or what to expect so I’m not sure what is normal. It’s the weekend so I’m unable to call my doctor. They sent me home with pain pills and five days worth of pills to treat an infection in case I got one during and after surgery. Also, last night I also woke up and felt nauseas. I do not have a fever though.

I've had botox done twice so far. First time I had some hesitency afterwards, but nothing long term....but the second time I had it done, i noticed within a weeks time, I was unable to void hardly at all. I then got a really bad infection & thus ended having to cath up to 12 times a day because I wasn't voiding. My sensation to void & my muscles were not cooperating... I'd definitely talk to your dr about this and maybe they can reccommend some sort of relaxant to help your muscles. Because what needs to happen when you need to void is "your bladder contracts, and your pelvic floor muscles relax pushing the urine out" well botox essentially stops the contractions to a point depending on where your baldder was injected. Good luck & feel free to message me if you have any questions. Ive been through this all, so i know how frustrating it can be.

Thank you. I ended up going to Urgent Care saturday night and they talked with the on call urologist and he said everything sounded normal and I was ok because I emptied to 160cc. I ended up being able to get more out sunday so things are moving along. Its just irritating and scary with everything settling down. I'm praying this gets me a long ways.

HI When I tried to explain before that my doctor said that botox was the wrong answer for your bladder, I got dogged pretty bad thru pm's.. because I stated that it was used for cosmetic purposes only and it is only a temperory fix for a problem. It is not long term and not only will it temperorly numb the nerves it can permenatly damage them and cause severe rentention in your bladder. I looked it up in thru the FDA approvals and NO where in here is it approved for IC. On April 15, 2002, the FDA approved the use of Botox injections to temporarily reduce the appearance of frown lines and wrinkles between the eyebrows. The product has already been widely used for this purpose by dermatologists, plastic surgeons, and other doctors. But this approval formally gives the manufacturer permission to market it as a wrinkle reducer. Here is more information about the approval. Brand name: Botox Cosmetic Generic name: Botulinium Toxin Type A Maker: Allergan, Inc., of Irvine, Calif. How it is used: Botox is a toxin produced by the bacterium Clostridium botulinum. When a purified form of the toxin is injected into the muscles, it blocks the production of a chemical called acetylcholine, which normally causes the muscles to contract and cause wrinkles. As a result, the injection temporarily paralyzes or weakens the affected muscles. Botox is available only by prescription and must only be used under strict medical supervision. Botox was first approved in 1989 to treat eye muscle disorders and in 2000 to treat a nerve disorder called cervical dystonia,which causes severe neck and shoulder contractions. Basis of approval: The FDA based its approval for this new use of Botox on several clinical trials involving a total of 405 people with moderate-to-severe frown lines between the eyebrows (known as glabellar lines) who were injected with Botox or a placebo. After 30 days, the great majority of researchers and patients said the frown lines improved or were nonexistent. Very few people in the placebo group saw similar results. The appearance of these frown lines was reduced for up to 120 days in the Botox group. It's recommended that the drug be used no more frequently than once every three months and the lowest possible dose should be used. Concerns: The most common side effects after injection were headache, respiratory infection, flu symptoms, droopy eyelids, and nausea. Less than 3% of patients reported more severe reactions such as pain in the face, redness at the injection site, and muscle weakness. These symptoms were usually temporary, but could last several months.

contents: Brat i wonder if these doc's that use Botox for treatment are paid by the company to push this product? I would hate to think that my doc only gave me this because of a paycheck!!!!! Leslie

If one is considering the use of Botox, one might want to check with the centers that are using Botox in the bladder for treatment of spasms, incontinence, urgency and frequency, and pain relief--which is, incidentally, a secondary outcome. To my knowledge, there is no evidence that confirms the use of Botox in the bladder permanently damages muscles. It paralyzes muscles for a given period of time. If any of you have evidence to the contrary, could you please share it with us and cite the source(s). We all would be interested in that material and appreciate the sharing. How else can we learn? The problems that have occurred with Botox used anywhere, from what I have had explained, have happened when Botox gets in the hands of someone who is not trained to use it. A good example of this situation is the Botox cosmetic parties where the provider injecting the Botox is untrained or is improperly trained. My cousin is a reconstructive and plastic surgeon and has used Botox since it was first approved by the FDA for cosmetic purposes. We have discussed Botox in detail. He does not concur with the concept of muscle damage. Of course, Botox is temporary; it disappears from your body in 4 to 6 months after injection. But aren't bladder instills temporary, too? I would hate to think that the statements made herein would discourage the use of Botox by someone who really could benefit from its experimental bladder use. Everyday, we read, on these boards, someone stating that he or she is trying an experimental procedure or waiting to take part in an experimental trial. All of us may want to keep in mind that Elmiron and DMSO are the only two IC specific treatments/ medications approved by the FDA. I have not seen where Neurontin and many other frequently used medications are FDA approved for IC use. IC is frustrating to treat, and numerous other medications--not FDA approved--have, are, and will be used for its treatment. Why not stay open minded about Botox. It may provide yet another treatment protocol for IC. Before the use of any experimental procedure, one needs to read and weigh all of the pro's and con's and make his or her own person decision. I have done this with any IC treatment protocol that I have chosen. [I will honestly report that I have had both successes and failures.] This education process should, in my opinion, include discussions with physicians and patients who have used Botox for cosmetic purposes, back procedures, and bladder procedures.

contents: Ok here are my thoughts on the Botox injections. There are so many things out there for IC patients to try to help alleviate the pain, urgency and frequency. But for those of us who the traditional therapies don't work on, we tend to open our minds a little bit. If this Botox can give me 1 ounce of relief, it's worth it. Because nothing else has helped. I don't want to spend the rest of my life on narcotic pain therapy. I'm 26 years old and eventually, I will come to a point where there is nothing else that is going to help me as far as pain control. Everything that we put into our bodies to try to help with this miserable disease has it's pro's and con's. But like all of those things, like the interstim, and the instills, they are all a ray of hope for us. That is what Botox is. It's a ray of hope for those people who have tried everything else with no luck. Or maybe they aren't ready for something like the interstim yet and want to try Botox as a last ditch effort to relieve their symptoms before going to something as invasive as the interstim. I think if it helps you, thats wonderful, if it doesn't, than it doesn't. Not much we can do then other than go to something else to try as another ray of hope to be able to be ourselves again. Just like the whole Coral Calcium debate. Some wouldn't go a day without it, others feel it's a big crock of bull that some company is just trying to make money on a false hope. I believe I have had IC for about 8 years now. Diagnosed for 2. And honestly, I feel no better today than I did 8 years ago. I think we all have this feeling inside of us that prays the next thing we try will fix everything. When you live your life, day to day, in the toilet, in severe pain, you tend to try anything and everything that says it will help you. I have done it myself. I tried everything out there that said it would help me, only to be disappointed when it didn't help. So, I think if my insurance covered Botox, I would try it. You never know until you try something if it's going to work or not. I'm very glad for those people who it has helped, and in just those few people, it has made a world of difference in their lives. So, why not try it??? If after a few treatments, you have no relief, stop. I'm sure the damage Botox may cause is after years of treatments. I don't think the FDA would approve a drug that damaged your nerves after a few treatments. No, right now, Botox is not approved for bladder use, but, they are in the process from what I understand. I think that if it helps even half of the people who do the treatments, it's worth it. heck, Elmiron, the ONLY drug FDA approved for IC, doesn't help everyone. Even the instills. Lets take a medication that will literally burn the skin off and stick it in our bladders. I'm sure after so many years of that, it will have SOME negative impact. But, during those several years, some patients find relief of their symptoms. As I said before, if it helps, go for it!

contents: Brat, go back to your original post. What you copied said: --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- As a result, the injection temporarily paralyzes or weakens the affected muscles. --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Notice the adverb modifier "temporarily;" it modifiers both "paralyze" and/or "weaken." I interpret this statement to read temporarily weakens muscles. I have not seen anyone who has had botox post that they have had permanent weakening of muscles. The temporary paralysis of muscles does occur because there is no way to separate out the nerve endings in the bladder from the muscles. Everything does reverse in 4 to 6 months. I have done rather extensive research on the use of botox in the bladder. Why don't we wait and get this question fully answered by our botox guest speaker?

I have several questions where I would like to have input. I would like to hear first-hand experiences from actual botox participants. I am not interested in speculation. How immediate is the bladder spasm, urgency and frequency relief? Does pain relief always occur, or is it an occasional by product of the botox injections? Does the amount of botox used make a difference in a person's response? Or is this very individualized like our responses are to different food items? I have noticed that urine retention is mentioned in some of the postings on this board. What amount of TEMPORARY retention occurs during the first month to two months following botox injections? Both bladder nerve endings and muscles are injected, because, as I understand the research, you can't totally separate out the two when doing the injections. My perception is that this may lead to some retention. Is the retention severe enough at first that catheterization becomes necessary? If so, for how long? Again, does the amount of botox used make a difference in the level of urine retention? Since botox disappears from one's system in 4-6 months, how long does the botox provide relief? Is there relief in areas other than bladder spasms, urgency, frequency, and pain. What about urethral and pelvic floor relief? I am sure our guest lecturer will address all of this; however, I would like to hear about personal experiences so that I can put the guest lecturer's material into a better perspective.

contents: Like anyone else suffering from IC, i was pretty willing to try anything to get my life back. Sometimes there is a tendency to want to feel normal again that you dont do all the drug history homework that one is supposed to do. I tried DMSO for a year and it helped me and many people are opposed to that. But if you are laying in bed 24/7 and have no life the risk of a certain drug is something you have to weigh. And I mean a legal prescribed drug. I would be willing to try botox but, in many cases, it may not be covered by uros as it is not approved for IC. There would be a problem there as I have spent most of my money during the past 10 years on meds, bills related to IC. But if botox were approved for IC use I would try it. I do well on the elavil but still have to deal with the side effects.

I had reached a point where I didn't even care if it was safe, I was so desperate although I did research botox first, it was worth it to me to try it. Unfortunately I bombed on the botox but I had to try it. I have doubts about clorpactin too but since I've had little quality of life this past year, I'm having to try some things on my "never" list.

contents: I believe everybody is entitled to their opinion. I have had Botox done five times and would just like to take a few minutes to share some facts (in my case only). I had tried every treatment option available to me and had been in debilitating pain for nearly 10 years. In September, 2002 I was offered a new experimental (off-label) treatment for IC. In 2002 there was very little known about this procedure and I certainly was more then a little apprehensive about the entire ideal of Botox in my bladder. This very new urologist explained everything to me. He explained the risks and possible side effects, and he explained the possible benefits. He left the decision totally up to me and did not try to push me into doing anything. He simply listed my options, explained each thing about each and I made the decision. In fact, he wasn't too sure it would help me and expressed his thoughts to me. I had the first round (200 units all in the bladder) in Oct 2002 and I have to say that it was probably the least effective treatment of them all. I did have problems with retention. Other then the retention - the botox did not make my problems worst. I just used a catheter and was thrilled to have some releif. The second time, the uro used 100 units and it was much easier and I didn't have much retention. I had much better results but still had problems with urethra spasms, pelvic floor spasms, and vulvodynia. The third time, the uro used about 150 units and injected the bladder and urethra. Again better results then the first two rounds. Hardly any retention and more pain releif. The forth time, the uro used 200 units and injected my bladder, urethra, and pelvic floor muscles. Let me tell you, I had to pester him for months about trying it on the pelvic floor. But in May 2005, he finally agreed and injected the pelvic floor too. For the first time in years I had much less vaginal swelling and irritation. Just that made the entire procedure worth it. During the last three years I completely replaced all my doctors treating me and picked a team of doctors that all know about IC and related conditions. All of these doctors are some of the best in Texas. My internist, pain specialist, and gynecologist all believe that I am doing the right thing for me. They all support the urologists treatment plan and support my decision to continue the treatment. In August 2004, I went to my gynecologist about vestibulitis and vulvodynia problems. This was the first time I had gone to see her (she specializes in vulvo-vaginal disorders). I explained to her all of my problems with pelvic pain and about the botox. How it benefits me and what problems I was still having. She told me about a lecture she had gone to where they were using botox for vestibulitis and vulvodynia. She suggested that I speak to my uro about having the injections for it when I have the injections for the bladder the next time. I talked to him and he was a little hesitant but did agree that it wouldn't hurt me and probably wouldn't be as effective as in the bladder. Nov 19, 2004 I had round five done and had 300 units injected in my bladder, urethra, pelvic floor muscles, and this time the vestibule and bartolin glands. With help from the urolgoist, pain management specialist and gynecologist I know how to address the increased symtoms during intimacy and for the first time in 12 years I have been able to have almost completely pain free intimacy with my husband. I still have pain/problems from the IC, vulvodynia, vestibulitis, pudendal neuralgia, and PFD. I will never be completely pain free but I can manage my symtoms and for the most part live a fairly functionable life. I prayed for a doctor that would work with me and help me anyway he could. I thank God for answering my prayers with leading me to a team of doctors that filled that request. I think the most important thing is that you find a team of doctors that can work together and they with your input figure out what is best for you. I tell people not to expect miracles when trying botox. It may take a few times to figure out your trigger points and tailor the procedure to fit your needs. That's the best thing about Botox, it is injections and they can inject in trigger points and each time eliminate more til they find the right points that best accommodates you. It does offend me that anybody would suggest that my uro would decide to do a procedure based on payment from the botox company. I believe my uro to be an honest and compasionate doctor. I truly believes he only treats me with options that he believes is best for me. I am very grateful that he is willing to hang in there with me and alter his treatment to my needs. Cindy Last edited by CinSin; 12-07-2004, 03:54 AM.
The bulk of this discussion took place a year ago so some of the opinions posted here may have changed - I don't know. I didn't notice the dates at first, but these are quite old.

I've been virtually symptom free and able to eat & drink whatever I'd like for about 8 years now. ***************************** “We who lived in concentration camps can remember the men who walked through the huts comforting others, giving away their last piece of bread. Thank you to each & every one of you who contributed to this ''Botox Question". We will always have different people with different exeriments & different knowledge. I embrace any info give here to help weigh up the pros & cons. I have just come back from my support meeting today in Melbourne where Botox was a major disscussion there. I had been to a URO that was completley against the idea of botox, his concern is what long term damange botox does to the bladder??? And I guess they carn't answer that yet as they don't know? However the DR whom spoke at our support meeting was quiet for, or at least using it as an IC experiment. And no this is by any means a cure & would have to be repeated every 6-9months. I thank you Brat for shareing your story & info with us, along with everyone else who had their say. This is one place where EVERYONE should be able to express their thoughts, opions & knowledge. I know that I am one person who receives it with open arms. Thank you Andrea
WOW I also just noticed that the original post was posted along time ago. I guess as Botox is now being so widely looked at it is a good time to talk about it again. Andrea

Botox Injections In 2004 I had the Interstim Implant and it didn't work for me. It was a very painful operation to boot, let me tell you and I'm a pretty tough old gal! But when ya have to go to the bathroom every 5 minutes and sleep on the toilet, one will try anything, and I did. I am having it removed July 22nd of this year. My doctor, who is well known all over the US, Dr. Kenneth Peters, is a very recognized doctor in his field of experimenting with IC and has recommended me for a Botox Trial and I have said yes. He did warn me of the con's. That I may need a Catheter if it doesn't work, but if I can't feel the catheter because I'm frozen down there, then I welcome a Catheter!!!! The reason I don't wear one now is because it burns me like heck and it's the most painful thing I've ever had attached to me. I actually have been wanting my bladder removed in order to have a life. When Dr. Peter's told me that I may have to have injections twice a year because Botox doesn't last long, and acted so sad about it, I laughed and told him that one day would be a miracle for me. Because I never know when I will have a bad day (having to go to the bathroom every 5 minutes and this is the truth, not an exaggeration, and on good days about every 20 minutes), whenever I'd go shopping, I'd have to leave the shopping cart, only to find it gone when I returned. When I asked what happened to it the clerks would say, they thought I abandoned it. Tired of that happening all the time, I began wearing diapers. Plus being stuck in traffic these days is not fun when you have to go!!! So Depends have been my new best friend and I have learned to use them, no matter where I am and nobody knows what's going on. But I still can't do everything that I want to do. You can only go so many times in a diaper. The same with a Catheter. It needs changing. And this is fine. It's better than nothing. But to be able to go for an hour or two, and the thought of only a few times a day is like a Fairy tale to me. My friend of mine came over the other day and visited for about 5 hours. She never had to go once. And didn't even have to go when she left. I looked at her like she was a rock star. I was in "Awe" of her. Because I am in the "Public Eye", or was before this happened, I am willing to try anything in order to get back into the career I once had. So Botox is very tempting to me to say the least. What scares me is this. What happens if I get Botox and then I can't go, because the muscles won't work as they did before, and I have the same feeling that I have to go! But now I can't. The only way I can relieve that feeling, that pressure, is to go. But what happens if I can't now and I still have that same feeling? I know I'd go crazy. It would be more than I could bare! But I have awhile yet to make up my mind. If anyone has had Botox Injections, I'd like to read about it. Your experience. Were you as bad as me? If so...how are you now! Gosh if only I knew ahead of time. Scary not to know what will happen. Thanks Last edited by Nancy178; 06-27-2009, 05:20 AM.

Botox Nancy178... I had botox on 5/11, and for me, it did not work. I hesitate to discourage you from trying it though, since I have heard it works very well for a lot of people. The week after I had it was one of the worst I can remember, and now, 7 weeks later, my frequency is unchanged (I average from 30-40 times per day), and I do have to "force" myself to go. I believe this has served to further irritate my bladder. I am taking Flexeril among other meds, and it does not seem to helping me a lot. I also take vicodin, darcalma.....I tried Elmiron with no success and have done instills of Elmiron as well as DMSO in the past with minimal success. I am now altering my diet to try to get some relief. In addition to typical IC triggers, I am in the process of eliminating gluten to see if that may also be a trigger for me. Basically, nothing much has given me relief, so I am really just treating my symptoms now. I would consider the Interstim, but because I can never have an MRI if I get it, that makes it quite a huge decision for me. Pelvic floor therapy is also my next move to see if that may help. I rarely if ever complain or vent to anyone......whew, feels good to get this off of my chest!!

Thank You Thank you for responding. After reading several comments on here regarding their experiences with Botox, or what they've heard, and now yours, I have decided against it. I have learned to live with what I have. My life may not be what it once use to be, but I have adapted. As long as Depends work and allows me to leave the home and do some things, that's better than nothing. I'm so sorry to hear this from you because I can only imagine what you are going through. I'm so sorry! Thank you for sharing. BTW, Interstim has not worked for me, and it is a very painful operation!!! Please take care. Nancy

Botox Thanks Nancy! Like I said, I think there are a lot of others who have found success with Botox, I just don't happen to be one of them. I have a cornucopia of meds that I take, and I am kind of getting sick of taking them! Pyridium, Darcalma, Flexeril, Vistaril, Elmiron, Vicodin for pain (which is daily now), Benadryl.....blah blah! I have to say that I function quite well, and don't really talk to many people about the fact that I have IC, since most people don't understand it, and some actually think it is funny. Still, it is hard to train without being sure I can find a restroom at least every 30 minutes or so. Maybe I need the Depends too! Seriously, you do what you have to, right??? Take care!! J

Im gonna have to agree with you!! Originally posted by tigger_gal HI When I tried to explain before that my doctor said that botox was the wrong answer for your bladder, I got dogged pretty bad thru pm's.. because I stated that it was used for cosmetic purposes only and it is only a temperory fix for a problem. It is not long term and not only will it temperorly numb the nerves it can permenatly damage them and cause severe rentention in your bladder. I looked it up in thru the FDA approvals and NO where in here is it approved for IC. On April 15, 2002, the FDA approved the use of Botox injections to temporarily reduce the appearance of frown lines and wrinkles between the eyebrows. The product has already been widely used for this purpose by dermatologists, plastic surgeons, and other doctors. But this approval formally gives the manufacturer permission to market it as a wrinkle reducer. Here is more information about the approval. Brand name: Botox Cosmetic Generic name: Botulinium Toxin Type A Maker: Allergan, Inc., of Irvine, Calif. How it is used: Botox is a toxin produced by the bacterium Clostridium botulinum. When a purified form of the toxin is injected into the muscles, it blocks the production of a chemical called acetylcholine, which normally causes the muscles to contract and cause wrinkles. As a result, the injection temporarily paralyzes or weakens the affected muscles. Botox is available only by prescription and must only be used under strict medical supervision. Botox was first approved in 1989 to treat eye muscle disorders and in 2000 to treat a nerve disorder called cervical dystonia,which causes severe neck and shoulder contractions. Basis of approval: The FDA based its approval for this new use of Botox on several clinical trials involving a total of 405 people with moderate-to-severe frown lines between the eyebrows (known as glabellar lines) who were injected with Botox or a placebo. After 30 days, the great majority of researchers and patients said the frown lines improved or were nonexistent. Very few people in the placebo group saw similar results. The appearance of these frown lines was reduced for up to 120 days in the Botox group. It's recommended that the drug be used no more frequently than once every three months and the lowest possible dose should be used. Concerns: The most common side effects after injection were headache, respiratory infection, flu symptoms, droopy eyelids, and nausea. Less than 3% of patients reported more severe reactions such as pain in the face, redness at the injection site, and muscle weakness. These symptoms were usually temporary, but could last several months.

contents: My urologist, who is a specialist in female cases of IC, does not recommend Botox injections. It's a super controversial treatment that can actually really screw things up if not done properly... and apparently, it's hard to do it properly. The idea of Botox anywhere really freaks me out now that I did my own research on it too as well as heard his opinion. I am all for getting some relief- that's why I got my left side of the spinal nerve blocks done and I'm getting the right side done in two weeks- but I am not going to go the Botox route. It's too much of a risky treatment for me. That said, if you do decide to get the Botox injections, I hope they are done properly by someone who is experienced in doing them, and that they help you. Best of luck!

contents: Hey Teresa! I got the Botox into my pelvic floor, not my abdomen (but I do know some people get simple trigger point injections into the lower abdomen). So yeah- I got my TPI of Botox into my pelvic floor, which meant vaginally (I have tried every treatment before this over a period of a year and a half and I have extremely severe PFD) last Thursday and the recovery from the procedure has been a little rough, but I'm now recovered and its already kicking in (the Botox takes about a week to start working). This is by FAR the most relief I have EVER had, I literally was crying in happiness when I got to go with a normal urine stream without a catheter!!!! It has helped a lot with the constant spasming and this is just the start- I will continue to update as a little bit more time passes about the continued results. They only did one vial VERY diluted- 10 injections, so they can see how I respond to this initial try. I would never do this treatment without thoroughly discussing it with your doctor- your questions, concerns, every detail of the procdure (how many injections, how much botox) finding out your doctor's experience using it, etc. I called many times beforehand and had lots of concersaqtions to address each of my questions and concerns. I had two different doctors finally reach to the idea of the botox treatment for me. They would not consider it until I tried other therapies first- physical therapy, success with trigger points, oral meds, etc over a long period of time. I will continue to update over the next few days! -Katie Last edited by Katie63086; 03-18-2010, 10:32 AM.

I am going to try trigger point injections at my next uro appt (will be receiving them in my abdomen only). I am nervous but hopeful because I don't seem to be making much progress with PT alone.

contents: I wanted to clarify that acupuncture is very different than trigger point injections. It is my understanding that TPIs go much deeper down into the muscle whereas acupuncture needles are very superficial (I've had acupuncture done before). I also would be leery of going to an acupuncturist who wasn't trained/experienced in pelvic floor issues and trigger points. I don't think the practitioner could hurt you but they just may not be able to help you either. My pain is all around my bladder from the outside of my abdomen and around my urethra. I really don't have any internal vaginal pain therefore my TPIs will be done externally on the abdomen.

Thank you very much for your post. I just keep thinking that there are some people who have been in remission for really long lengths of time. One lady on here said 15 years. I have talked to others who said their symptoms appeared (quickly and acutely) and then they went away. One lady that works with my step-father talked to me and told me she had this too and then about 30 years ago a uro "painted her bladder" - that's what he called it. I think it was some kind of instil - she did the treatments monthly for about 3 months and she has never had a reoccurance. I really wish more people like that would post on here, but they have moved on and don't give IC a second thought. It is interesting, though, when I talked to her she has some other autoimmune type things going on now - she is now in her 60's. My step-father says that no matter what she is going through she does a great job at work. I want to have faith that if some people can go into such long remissions that I can too.

Has anyone had botox injections? are these the trigger point injections? if not what are they injecting you with? Thanks Nyeema

I've had treatment with botox twice now - most recently the end of December. My urologist said it can be done as often as every 6 months. What he did was cystoscopy with bladder distention and injected the botox while "in there". I was put under an "altered state of consciousness", which for me, is safer than general anesthesia and the same as far as having no recollection of any part of the procedure. Recovery was better for me the first time. This time I was in the middle of my worst flare ever along with a horrible flare of IBS. Not sure how I feel about the results this time. Pain has been the same both times, but it has helped with my frequency of urination. I still get the painful sensation to empty my bladder, but at the same time feel like I can hold it a little longer. Only getting up a few times during the night as opposed to countless times. For me, even a little help is worth it.

contents: I asked the last Uro at Hopkins about having Botox. I was told that my insurance would not cover it and that to have it done at Hopkins would be about 15,000. The other alternative offered was for about 5,000 I could have it done in office with no anesthesia if I thought I could stay on the table LOL. I was all excited about Botox but the more I've read and the cost it would be the less excited I am to try. This disease has almost taken me broke already, all the money I have spent on it and all the business not done because of it, the cost/benefit analysis of trying to botox does not look great. Perhaps I'll try leeches next - they are cheap and they are cheap.

Injecting botulinum toxin (Botox) to treat intrinsic or allergic rhinitis may be a thing of the past as researchers have now shown that sponges soaked in Botox are equally effective in treating the condition. The research published in BioMed Central's open access journal Head & Face Medicine offers a potential needle-free treatment to the millions of people who suffer from rhinitis.... More here: http://www.newsmaxhealth.com/health_stories/Botox_allergic_rhinitis/2009/10/16/273120.html

contents: I was here probably a couple of months ago - I had 170 units of Botox injected into my bladder and vagina. For the first three weeks I had problems with retention which my uro said was due to the pelvic muscles adjusting from the Botox, but I am not sure about that. I did a lot of research and even e-mailed a very well-known urologist who does a lot of Botox and he said he only puts 100 units of Botox into the bladder or it causes retention. Then once that calmed down I started to have more pain than prior to the Botox so I submitted a sample for testing to see if I had an infection. I never did find out what the bacteria was just that it was gram negative and my GP said I had a mild uti and put me on Cipro 500 twice a day for one week. I continued to have a lot of pain and after waiting a few days I submitted another sample to the lab. This time the result indicated a more serious infection, except due to their being a number of organisms the lab put it down as a "contaminant". My GP said to wait a few days again and submit another sample. This time the result again was a serious infection with the bacteria Klebsiella and my GP put me on Cipro again for one week. Nothing has changed, except that I am getting very frustrated and feel like I am in a three-ring circus! This time the lab was faxing older results to my GP. So once again I have submitted a urine sample to the lab and as I write this I am waiting to hear from my GP what the culture shows. I am wondering if any others who have had Botox into the bladder and vagina have experienced utis. I did a lot of research on Botox and talked to a number of ICers who had Botox and never did I hear any one say anything about Botox and bladder infections. Last weekend I was on the net looking into aftereffects of Botox and came across a well-known website which I don't know if I should give here, but they said regarding Botox "What Are The Risks": * Bladder infections occur in some patients. These may be caused by the procedure itself, or may be due to incomplete bladder emptying as the Botox takes effect. Notify your doctor if you are concerned about a possible infection. * Up to 1 in 5 patients may require temporary self-catheterization to help the bladder empty. * Other very rare potential adverse events, which have not been described following bladder injections may include: * Allergic reaction, including anaphylaxis; * Erythema multiforme (a severe skin rash); and, * Generalized weakness. It has been almost three months since I had my first Botox injections into the bladder and vagina and I have to say I have felt miserable and in a lot more pain. Due to this I have also experienced depression most of the time. I had high hopes for the Botox helping with the bladder and pelvic pain I have been living with for over thirty years. As someone else said, I feel this is another treatment that has failed as I cannot see going through this suffering again. In the site I mentioned above, they also said: "... the treatment effect will commonly last 3 to 6 months, and sometimes up to 8 or 9 months." This does not make me a happy camper with the possibility of dealing with more pain and more infections for months to come! Louise

No responses!! I AM WONDERING WHY THERE ARE NO RESPONSES TO MY MESSAGE!! IF THOSE OF YOU WHO HAD BOTOX INJECTIONS ARE NOT HAVING UTIS AS WELL, YOU ARE VERY LUCKY. HAVING IC IS ONE THING, HAVING BOTOX IS ANOTHER MISERY BUT THEN ADDING SEVERE UTIS (LIKE WITH KLEBSIELLA AS THE BACTERIA CAUSING THE INFECTION) HAS MADE MY LIFE AND THAT OF MY LIFE A LIVING H**L!!! I UNDERSTAND THAT YOU COULD BE HAVING OTHER MEDICAL PROBLEMS OF YOU OWN AT THE SAME TIME SO I AM TOTALLY COMPASSIONATE IN THAT REGARD. LOUISE

Botox I had botox injected into my bladder wall in Dec 2008, I have had no problems with uti's however, I now have a mild rectal prolapse, definately caused from straining with peeing due to the botox.

It may or may of not been caused by botox. take care

oh also when i got botox only into my pelvic floor muscles my bladder improved heappppppppps without the need for botox in the bladder...... maybe you need to just get botox into the muscles not the actaul bladder................if it is the botox then it will wear off and your pain will improve even thou it must be so hard to believe that........ have you tried low dose valium that may help you alot!! oh also botox gave me a flare intially too and it took me about three months to till all of a sudden i felt great so dont give up you may be like me a slow to change person..........my second botox gave me a year of great pain free PFD and improved bladder...............but alais cant have more while im trying for bubs..............

I had botox done about a month ago. I have had an UTI and bladder infection since and some trouble totally emptying my bladder. I have noticed some improvements in some areas, like less bathroom trips. Not as many flares. But I have gained 15 lbs and my tummy is constantly bloated. Did anyone else notice this? Do u think trying again might make it better over time?

I am scheduled for my botox procedure next Friday, October 16, 2009. I am in a lot pain and seeking anything to help with the pain.....but I don't like what I am reading. I do know everyone reacts differently to different procedures and medication. Pray for me. Love & Peace Trinkles

Hi, All-- I'm new to this site and haven't even received an IC diagnosis. After my first visit to a urologist, the Dx is urinary frequency and urge incontinence, but I don't have pain (luckily!), so I'm not sure that I have IC. I'm scheduled for urodynamics testing next week, and my doc will wait to get those results, but already at my first appt. she was talking about Botox injections into the bladder wall (how do they do that?) as a potentially good treatment for me. (All of the meds normally used to treat this are apparently contraindicated in my case due to serious digestive issues that I have.) I'm concerned by the little I've read on this site about Botox reactions, especially retention and UTIs. I have severe rheumatoid arthritis, so the flexibility and hand-dexterity needed to cath myself are out of the question--someone else would have to do it for me. And because I'm immuno-suppressed, I'm at high risk for serious infection. Any thoughts? Thanks so much!

thanks! Bri, Thanks so much for your thorough and thoughtful post. I am not new to my symptoms but am new to the whole field of urology, so this is all very helpful My symptoms are needing to urinate pretty often (at least 15-16 times per 24-hr period, usually between 2-11 cc output) and that I usually have very little warning (sometimes as much as 10 mn, sometimes much less) before I have to go really badly, meaning that I have accidents. (I always wear pantiliners.) The symptoms have become more severe over the past few years because I have to drink a lot in the a.m. and at night to help with digestive issues that are quite severe w/o my constant monitoring: gastroparesis, or very slow gut motility, such that I can't even digest a lot of foods; and constipation-dominant irritable bowel syndrome, such that I have to take laxatives [OKed by gastro doc] every morning and evening and then still must drink lots of fluids all day to allow me to be able to eat anything more than, say, cream of wheat. So I know that part of my trouble could be relieved if I drank less, but I can't do that if I want to stay out of the hospital b/c of stomach ulcers or bowel impaction. One great thing: I never have any pain, and I haven't had a UTI since childhood. (The latter is a very good thing--because of the immunosuppressants I'm on to treat severe childhood-onset rheumatoid arthritis, getting an infection could be life-threatening for me. This is why the posts about retention and UTIs with Botox worried me.) It was my understanding, from what my urologist said, that most of the medicines used to treat my symptoms would exacerbate my already-difficult-to-manage gastroparesis and severe constipation. But she didn't rule anything out and said she would certainly want to consult with my gastro doc. The only other things she mentioned were the Botox and a pacemaker (the latter she thought would also be a concern given my infection risk). So I was surprised to go to this board and see that Botox is considered experimental. However, I've had only one meeting with her--I have the urodynamics test next week--so we haven't really had a chance to talk seriously about my options. I checked out the diet site, and most of the "no-nos" are things I don't eat anyway, like caffeine, soda, artificial sweeteners. The two things on the list that I do use and probably need to keep taking are orange juice (fortified with calcium--it's one of the best ways to get calcium for me given my digestive issues, and OJ bothers my stomach less than apple juice) and multivitamins (children's chewable--that's all my stomach can handle). I haven't noticed that I'm better at times when I haven't had either of those things, but I can talk to my doctor about it. Otherwise, I'm hesitant to impose more restrictions on my diet since I'm already so limited by my gastroparesis (I can only tolerate a few kinds of cooked vegetables, and about the only raw fruit I can handle these days is apples.) I have another question: can you tell me about the urodynamics test? I'm a bit anxious about it because, while I'm VERY familiar with all sorts of medical tests and procedures, ones of this nature can be hard for me because of a childhood history of sexual trauma, some of it in a medical setting. I've already told my doctor I'll need to have female clinicians only performing the test and am hoping they can accommodate that. Thanks again so very much for all of your help! Aly

hi i have had botox for pelvic floor dysfunction so i had it injected into my pelvic muscles not my bladder...........i think you should be checked for PFD as well as your issues may be from PFD rather than IC.........I have heaps of bladder issues but i noticed that when i got the botox for PFD my bladder improved heaps. the botox saved my life and i cant reccomend it enough for PFD as in the pelvic muscles........I do however feel now that i do have IC as well but not severe mostly influenced by my PFD. urodynamics is not to scarey thou embarassing a bit.........they catherise you with and a wire to pick up readings and of cause a tube for urine flow etc...they pump you with water to see your compacity and then they let you pee in private in a toilet thing so they can measures your urine flow (mine was poor with starting and stopping which is linked to PFD) and lets you know how much you let out, gives them an idea if you are retaining as well..........its over pretty quicklY. GOOD LUCK

Thanks so much, Bemmer-- I appreciate hearing about your experience and also letting me know what to expect with the urodynamics test. What sort of testing do they do for PFD? they went by my symptoms, internal exam and a biofeedback thing that tests your pelvic floor tension...........my reading was 63, normal is about 30ish..I had to fly to sydney for testing and treatment as they have no idea here in NZ..........i have now found a pelvic floor specilist she said i was the worst case she had ever had as my muscles were like bricks lol !! and that was after botox!!! anyway having said that my PFD is pretty much controled with occasional flares and the odd sorse symptoms of pelvic pain around that time of the month.. It sounds like you are on to it and jut focus on one thing at a time...get the urodynamics test done and see what results come back form that, they usually can tell you straight away well i gt told the same day.. take care and the test isnt to bad promise!!! ness

contents: Hi there, I also do not have pain, but horrible frequency and urgency. I have been like this for a year. I also am prone to uti's. Are you similar at all with your symptoms? My doctor tells me it is unlikely that I have IC because i respond very well to antibiotics. when i actually do have a bladder infection ( 1 out of 20) times i get the symptoms, the antibiotics make me feel like a normal human with no bladder problems.

Botox in Dec 08 I had botox injected into my bladder wall, in Dec 2008. They do it under general anaesthesia. So you dont feel anything, you are in and out the same day provided you dont have any complications. It did work for a while, I was able to sleep through the night, or maybe get up once, which was a great improvement over 5-6 times a night. Same thing during the day, less urge, less frequency. The negative to that is that I had to strain to pee, the botox ended up paralyzing the bladder wall so I had to strain alot. Now I have a mild rectal prolapse and I am worried that I might be starting to have a bladder prolapse (fullness in vagina, pain during intercourse, I hope not). So definately think it over and talk to others, its a good and bad thing. If I had to do it again I dont think the side effects the prolapse are worth it.

Hello Everyone, I had botox injections into the pelvic floor muscles and the bladder almost 2 weeks ago for retention. I was told that if it works, it would take 4-7 days to see improvement. Ever since the injections, my retention has gotten worse and I'm continually straining to get the urine out with every void. I guess I can add this to my list of treatments that have failed. karen, i would call your doc...but i was told that retention can be made worse (at first) w/botox b/c it relaxes your muscles TOO much. i haven't had it done (insurance won't pay), but that is how it was explained to me. if you are really having a hard time, pls call your doc. good luck!

contents: botox The first time I had the botox injections it took 2 weeks to heal and I went form peeing 30 times a day to 8. It lasted 4 months and it was great. we did it again 6 months after the first time. I now have retention because the muscles are relaxed way to much. I can not pee on my own at all. I have to cath 8 to 10 times a day. I also have a small bladder so this is why I cath so often. It has been 2 and 1/2 months and still no change. I have had 2 UTI's in this time frame once right after the botox injections and just resently because they tried to have me use a indwelling cath for a while to give my urethra a break due to complications but I ended up with a UTI. no fun. So botox can help but it also can cause other problems it all depends on the person. you should call you doctor because you may need to cath to get you bladder to empty all the way so you don't end up with a UTI. Lynn

contents: My IC got worse with Botox I had Botox Nov 29th,2007... my pain got much worse after the procedure. It was HORRIBLE! IC pain is bad enough, but it was double that. The pain went on until about Dec, 26th... almost one month and then settled back into the normal IC pain. I wish I would have known before, but it does work for some people. I also had retention. Not to the point of needing to cath, but it was pretty bad and has lingered about. I never used to have any sort of retention, but now I will have to urinate so bad, but can't. Weird. Anke

Botox good and bad I guess as with anything there are ups and downs, I had botox injected into my bladder in Dec 2008, and while it definately cut down on the amount of times I went to the bathroom especially at night, I went from up every two hours to actually sleeping 6-8 hours through the night. I have to strain to pee, and unfortunately after about 3 months of doing this I now have a rectal prolapse which I will have to have surgery to repair. So it stopped the frequency and urgency, but definately caused other problems. So good luck, and just beware of the risks prior to having it done.

karen, I had botox treatment about 1 year ago. My uro said that it was one of the best kept secrets for success with IC. i HAD TO PAY FOR IT SO i WAS REALLY LOOKING FORWARD TO THE POSITIVE RESULTS. I know it is sometimes really hard. Your not alone.

contents: Hi all; when I got into the Center for Pelvic Pain at Kaiser SF I couldn't find a lot here about how it was for other people. I'm going to post a seperate piece about my experience with the different docs, but I got botox in my pelvic floor muscles on Friday and would like to share here so that others thinking about it can have a resource. I would also love to hear from others who might be trying different techniques with this. So I had a 'twilight' anasthesia on Friday am and the procedure plus recovery took up most of the day. I woke up and dealt with the typical nausea etc. from the sedation, and was on my way home in the late afternoon. The injection sites didn't seem to get very tender until Saturday. As I write today it is Sunday afternoon and one of the sites is very slightly bruised, but other than that they are not bothering me. I should mention, my sites are where the thigh and buttock meet, right about at the panty line. I thought they were going to go thru my entire buttcheek!!! So I'm pretty pleased with how small they are- not a big deal at all. The first night was a little rough, I was probably up 6 or so times to pee. This could be because I got a lot of hydration in the recovery room at the hospital. On a side note, those people couldn't believe how often I was having to pee! I'm dragging around this IV that is giving me so much fluids my eyes are floating, and I kept having to explain to nurses that me peeing is why I'm even there for a procedure- it was pretty funny. Saturday was pretty low key, I had a relatively good day by my standards. I've used an ice pack on my rump and a heating pad on my belly for most of the weekend, and spent a lot of time on the couch. I've also taken some Ibuprofen a few times mainly for the injection sites. In the post-op area Dr. Gunter explained that within the week I should notice some change in pain/urge/freq. and to make sure to do the work with my PT within 8 days or so. I've already got an app. to do that, so we'll see what happens. In my case, Gunter thinks that the Botox and some more PT might be able to tidy up my pfd and hopefully resolve a lot of my muscular symptoms. If not, we'll be looking at the nerves. I hope my explanation sheds some light on the procedure for someone else- I was pretty nervous and couldn't find a lot of personal accounts about it. Anyway, that's my story! Thanks for reading!
signature: I had major surgery for a genetic rectal prolapse in Aug.05 which I believe links directly to my new Dx of....PFD! March.08. I'm working with a great PT named Zelda- I think the world of her I have the same feelings for the team at the Kaiser Center for Pelvic Pain in SF- amazing people. I've had a cystoscopy, 2 cat scans, falied a potassium test and have had about 50 clean UA's. This is my hot water bottle- the fish. I love him! I'm also a big fan of my "Electric Fish," the TENS unit. Very helpful in a flare! **I'm getting Botox next week!** I take: Oxybutynin 10mg Elmiron 100mg 3x day Gabapentin 300mg 3x day Antihistamine, CystaQ, Bc, & Thyroid (125mcg) nightly I've also taken: Detrol LA Oxybutynin patch and lower dose pill Amitryptiline and Nortryptiline

contents: Wow congrats and please keep us posted. I'm an hour north of SF in Santa Rosa. I had never really considered botox before but am anxious to hear how it turns out for you. I have a lot of pain when I bend over or squat and I'm starting to think permanently relaxing the muscles down there might be a good thing. Can you tell me more about the procedure exactly? Why did you have to go under anesthesia? I'm guessing because the needle goes real deep? Do they actually put the botox into your bladder or just the muscles surrounding it.
signature: The combo of things that brought me out of remission were artificial sweetners, liquid vitamins with citric acid, tight pants, lower body workouts and stopping all my allergy meds cold turkey during height of allergy season. I wish I knew back then what I know now about IC First IC symptoms Spring of 2000; In-office cystoscopy 2002 -negative; Remission until 2007 (yeah!!); Negative PST spring of 2007; IC diagnosed via Cystoscopy and Hydrodistention 7/24/07; Another In-office cystoscopy 4/9/10 with biopsy showed inflammation Treatments for my IC/PFD: Elmiron 400 mg per day; walking, Zyrtec, Claritin, Singulair, Neurontin 100 mg per day; Ice packs and laying down; About to try Cytotec Tried and Failed: Heat (always makes me worse!), Heparin instills -too painful for my urethra; Atarax (interfered with my quality of sleep first time, second time dried my bladder out; Elavil makes me sick, lidocaine patches (just plain don't work), Cystoprotek (only tried a couple months but didn't seem to do much), Aloe Vera caps, pelvic floor therapy, trigger point work flared me worse, Lyrica (bad stomach pain) Other Diagnoses: PFD 2009; Fibromyalgia 2006, Osteopenia 2012, GERD 2006, Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying) Anxiety: Other medications: Ambien, Dexilant, Ibuprofren, Necon 1/35, Voltaren gel for tailbone BABY GIRL 10/28/08 *** BABY BOY 7/8/11

contents: Hi Charisse- thanks for reading all of that! The anaesthesia is because the needle is over 12 inches long, and Dr. Gunter said that precision is key in this procedure. I'm very glad I didn't have to see it!! I am feeling amazing. My frequency is down to less than 10 times a day, when before it was sometimes over 20. The physical therapy is a very important part of it though. My pt says that the muscles may have been tight, but that doesn't mean they were strong. I'm doing a lot of home exercises to re-tone the muscles while they are still paralyzed. Another fun thing I learned- Botox has been used for over 20 years in the muscles of people who have had a stroke and had a hand or arm tighten up. They use the same techniques of paralyzing the muscle in order to retrain. I think that's fascinating. Oh, and the injections went into the muscles around my bladder, not into it. Hope you're having a pain free day!

I'm also a big fan of my "Electric Fish," the TENS unit. So you will continue with the physio therapist? is she doing biofeedback? what other types of therapy does she do if you do not mind me asking? I am very excited for you!! Sending well vibes. Peace Last edited by bluetou; 11-13-2008, 07:30 AM.

Yes I'm very interested to hear your continued progress as well. Do you know how often the procedure is to be repeated? Also you mention the frequency is down but what about pain? Pain and spasms are my main issue.

contents: Thank you ! I just wanted to thank you for writing about the clinic within the Kaiser SF. I did not even know it was there nor did my Doctor. After I read about it I called my dr and he sent the request. I have an appt on Jan 2nd. I am so excited about this. Ever since they changed the chronic pain clinic in Santa Clara and stopped using MD accupuncturist anymore I have felt like I was left out in the cold. I am especially looking forward to PT and learning even more ways to deal with all of these symptoms. I love this forum !!!!!!!!!!!!!!
signature: with love, Katherine IC onset 31 dx 37, dx with TMJ at 14, dx with IBS and PFD at 43 current meds; 0.05mg Levothroid ton of vitamins. (Glucosamine Condr. NO MSN, Niacin, Turmeric Force, 2000 IU Vitamin D3, Calcium Citrate, Anti-Oxident, Vit B complex) IC Diet ~ very limited Hot tub *** was on 500 mg Morphine a day for 9 years*** ***May 14th 2009= 2 year OFF morphine !!! *** *****THERE IS ALWAYS HOPE*****

contents: Jess, How are you feeling now? Hope life is good !

Hi all I just wanted to stop by and mention that I'm feeling fantastic Botox seems to have really made a difference. I've felt so good that I haven't even had to go in to the city to see Dr. Gunter- we've talked on the phone instead! Awesome! I've been seeing my PT every 2-3 weeks and doing lots of ball work. She's also charted my muscle tone using biofeedback equipment, which has been really helpful for a visual aide. Now I'm just hoping to slowly introduce more foods into my diet. The PP center has a specific IC diet that I've been following to the letter, and it's grueling. I tell my friends that I'm vice-less I think it really makes a difference though. So many things that I've tried to eat have really flared me within only a few hours- so not worth it! I'm also on month 5 of the Elmiron, which hasn't given me any awful side effects. I'm just going to keep doing what I'm doing! Yeah it's a lotta drugs and not a lot of foods, but I'm finally starting to feel like myself again. A big part of it for me is just accepting that this is a part of my life now. Thanks for being a part of this community and reading my bit!

contents: Jess I am so glad you are getting relief. Do you find the diet you are on, similar to what the IC network diet is posting? Just curious? Glad you are doing well. Again so glad you are feeling better, and feeling progress in your methods of healing. Peace blue

The diet is pretty similar, just a more definitive list. ie, I don't have a maybe column I am avoiding not only acidic foods, but foods that contain tyramine and tyrosine. These are amino acids naturally occurring in many foods, and that develop as certain foods age. So almost all cheese is out for me, which is tough because I loooved my cheeses. People taking MAOI inhibitors also avoid these amino acids by because the drug makes them incapable of properly digesting the amino acids, causing all sorts of bad reactions. It's very interesting and confusing all at once- I have not figured it all out at all, but I'm just doing what the list says and trying not to think about it too much. I did an elimination diet observing various lists when my symptoms first started, but after a year I had put a lot of things back in. When my symptoms got really bad again and I was finally seeing Gunter, doing their list knocked a lot of things out of my diet again, plus many new things that I hadn't avoided at all. Like avoiding all nuts except for almonds, peanuts, and pine nuts- I used to eat tons of pistachios Has anyone else heard about avoiding the amino acids? I think I read a bit about it in the IC cookbook by Beverly.

contents: Another update- I am still doing just great I am down to gabapentin, elmiron, oxybutynin, cystaQ, antihistamine for mast cells, my normal thryoid and bc pills, and a vitamin D supplement, and I've added prozac for a short term while I get some therapy to adjust that this disease will be with me for who knows how long. I haven't had to see my PT or any Dr. related to my IC in over 9 months now. I follow the diet as strictly as I can and take my pills without skipping any Food still messes me up when I cheat or make a mistake, which can be pretty frustrating, but it is a relief that it's down to just those times now. Hope all are having pain-free days I'm going to probably stay off the boards for awhile now- there is too much negativity here. PM me if you want to talk about anything I've posted!
